metadata.title: The Inevitable, or A Dialogue on Predestination and Free Will
metadata.latin_title: Inevitabile sive Dialogus de Praedestinatione et Libero Arbitrio
metadata.author: Honorius Augustodunensis
known_issues: Several Biblical citations appear to have errors in the original manuscript (e.g., Hebrews 1:33 which only has 14 verses; John 16:18 should be 13:18)
Acts 13:21 citation should be Acts 14:22 for the tribulations saying
Text at chunk 217 contains an ellipsis suggesting possible corruption or missing text in the original manuscript
Translation note: The text shows significant dependence on Anselm of Canterbury's thought, particularly his treatises on grace, free will, and predestination
Greek transliterations and technical theological vocabulary maintained throughout
Some sections contain very harsh characterizations of different groups (clergy, monks, nuns, women) that reflect medieval polemical style rather than balanced description
The dialogue format is pedagogical rather than adversarial, with the student progressively learning from the master
The citation to Luke 22:7 for the hairs of the head saying should be Luke 12:7
The dialogue continues but is cut off at the chunk boundary
The work ends abruptly with the Disciple's final blessing, suggesting this may be the complete conclusion
Several cross-references to Anselm's works are embedded in the Latin text
Matthew 10:6 cited for vineyard parable which is actually Matthew 20
Text ends abruptly mid-sentence in Psalm citation at column 1216D
Some marginal notes appear incorporated into the body text
Some biblical citations follow medieval conventions that differ from modern chapter-verse divisions

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Opening: The Brothers Request Teaching on Predestination
chunks.latin: INEVITABILE SIVE DE PRAEDESTINATIONE ET LIBERO ARBITRIO INTER MAGISTRUM ET DISCIPULUM DIALOGUS.
chunks.english: The Inevitable, or A Dialogue on Predestination and Free Will Between Master and Student

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISCIPULUS. Fratres in domo Dei ambulantes cum consensu, sunt pro tua salute orationi instantes: diligentiae quidem tuae, orationes; Clavi autem David Christo, gratiarum solvunt actiones: qui ob Genitricis suae merita, tot eis in Canticis, de ea per te reseravit mysteria. Ob hanc causam, et ob alia quae multis incognita, elucidans in laudem ejus, debitores fecisti.
chunks.english: Student: The brothers who walk in unity in God's house are praying earnestly for your wellbeing. They offer prayers for your diligence, and they give thanks to Christ, the Key of David, who through your work has unlocked so many mysteries about his Mother in the Song of Songs, because of her merits. For this reason, and for clarifying many other things unknown to most in praise of her, you have made them your debtors.
chunks.notes: The 'Key of David' is a messianic title from Isaiah 22:22 and Revelation 3:7. The reference to the Song of Songs commentary indicates Honorius's Marian exegetical work.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illorum ergo nunc fungor legatione, et ipsi summa deposcunt devotione, ut solvas eis nodum liberi arbitrii inextricabilem: Si soli Praedestinati (ut quidam aiunt) quidquid etiam fecerint, salvantur: liberum arbitrium penitus tolli aestimatur, et hi qui damnantur, non solum jam sine culpa, sed etiam injuste puniri putantur.
chunks.english: So now I act as their representative, and they ask with deepest devotion that you untangle for them the inextricable knot of free will. If only the predestined are saved, no matter what they do, as some people claim, then free will seems to be completely eliminated, and those who are damned appear to be punished not only without fault, but even unjustly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde rogo te, de hac re latius disserere; nec minimum scrupulum alicui hebeti vel caeco ultra relinquere.
chunks.english: Therefore I ask you to discuss this matter more fully, and not to leave even the smallest difficulty for anyone dull or blind to understanding.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAGISTER. Cum summi doctores de hac materia multa ediderunt opuscula, quid a me amplius poscitis qui ad comparationem illorum sum elinguis?
chunks.english: Master: Since the greatest teachers have published many treatises on this subject, why do you ask anything more from me? Compared to them, I am tongue-tied.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Illi quidem egregie disputaverunt: sed diversa (ut nobis videtur) sentientes, incertiores nos reddiderunt. Tu autem (ut verum fatear) facis nobis breviter quodammodo palpabile, quod ipsi longis tractatibus non fecerunt saltem conspicabile.
chunks.english: Student: They certainly argued brilliantly, but because they seem to hold different opinions, they left us more uncertain. You, however, to tell the truth, make tangible for us briefly what they did not make even visible in their lengthy treatises.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quod petis, non denego; quia non mea, sed quae sunt charitatis quaero. Phaleras verborum contemno, dum fratrum simplicitati consulo: lividos quoque dentes invidorum despicio: quia auxilium meum a Domino.
chunks.english: Master: I do not refuse what you ask, because I seek not my own interests but those of charity. I despise verbal ornaments when I am concerned with the simplicity of the brothers, and I also disregard the envious fangs of the jealous, because my help comes from the Lord.
chunks.notes: 'Phaleras' refers to decorative metalwork on horses, hence metaphorically verbal ornamentation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quaeso ut me vere insipientem sufferas, et ignorantiam per me sciscitantium, verbo veritatis corrigas.
chunks.english: Student: I ask that you bear with me, who am truly foolish, and correct with the word of truth the ignorance of those who inquire through me.
chunks.notes: Echoes Paul's second letter to the Corinthians, chapter eleven, verse nineteen: 'You gladly put up with fools.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Veritas se quaerentes de tenebris erroris liberabit, et luce suae claritatis illustrabit. Si enim insipientem te facit causa charitatis, occurret tibi sapientia cum lumine veritatis.
chunks.english: Master: Truth will free those who seek it from the darkness of error and illuminate them with the light of its clarity. For if the cause of charity makes you foolish, wisdom will meet you with the light of truth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DIS. Tres nodi nexu nimis perplexo se invicem complicant; quos vix aliquis transit, quin eis inhaereat. Unus, quo asseritur: quod soli praedestinati salvantur. Alter, quo astruitur: quod per solam gratiam homines salutem consequantur. Tertius, quo dicitur: quod solum liberum arbitrium salutem operetur.
chunks.english: Student: Three knots intertwine with one another in an exceedingly perplexing tangle, which scarcely anyone passes through without getting stuck in them. The first asserts that only the predestined are saved. The second maintains that people attain salvation through grace alone. The third says that free will alone accomplishes salvation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In primis itaque seca nodum Praedestinationis; qui quadam violentia, cardinem obligat totius quaestionis. Putant enim quidam, quod Praedestinatio ad electos solos pertineat, cum Apostolus dicat: Quos autem praedestinavit, hos et vocavit, justificavit, magnificavit.
chunks.english: So first cut through the knot of predestination, which with a certain force binds the hinge of the entire question. For some people think that predestination pertains only to the elect, since the Apostle says in his letter to the Romans, chapter eight, verses twenty-nine and thirty: 'Those whom he predestined, he also called, justified, and glorified.'
chunks.notes: Reference is to Romans 8:29-30.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde primitus obsecro, ut dicas, quid sit Praedestinatio.
chunks.english: Therefore I first beg you to say what predestination is.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Nihil aliud est Praedestinatio, quam pro meritis cujusque, vel ad gloriam, vel ad poenam, aeterna Dei praeparatio.
chunks.english: Master: Predestination is nothing other than God's eternal preparation of each person, according to their merits, either for glory or for punishment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Est autem gemina Praedestinatio; una, bonorum ad gloriam; altera malorum ad poenam.
chunks.english: There is a twofold predestination: one of the good to glory, the other of the wicked to punishment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quia quidam etiam de Providentia et Praescientia mussitant; quasi hae necessitatem omnibus inferant: sciendum est, quod nec Providentia aliquem compellit ad boni studium; nec Praedestinatio ullum violenter trabit ad vitae praemium. Similiter Praescientia neminum cogit ad peccatum; et Praedestinatio nullum impellit necessitate ad supplicium.
chunks.english: But because some people also grumble about providence and foreknowledge, as if these impose necessity on everyone, it must be understood that providence does not compel anyone to pursue the good, nor does predestination drag anyone by force to the reward of life. Likewise, foreknowledge forces no one to sin, and predestination drives no one by necessity to punishment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Porro qui praevisi sunt in bonis permansuri, ad gloriam sunt praedestinati: qui vero praesciti in malo vitam finituri, ad poenam sunt utique praeordinati.
chunks.english: Moreover, those who are foreseen as persevering in good are predestined to glory, while those who are foreknown as ending their lives in evil are certainly preordained to punishment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ergo quos Deus praescivit in Filium suum credituros, et mandata ejus voluntarie servaturos, hos ante saecula praedestinavit: hos suo tempore ad fidem vocavit: hos per Baptisma justificavit: hos virtutibus magnificavit: hos vasa misericordiae glorificavit.
chunks.english: Therefore those whom God foreknew would believe in his Son and would willingly keep his commandments, he predestined before the ages. He called them to faith in their own time, justified them through baptism, magnified them with virtues, and glorified them as vessels of mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quos autem minime facturos praescivit, hos a regno gloriae reprobavit; et vasa irae et contumeliae, apta ad poenam praedestinavit.
chunks.english: But those whom he foreknew would not do these things, he rejected from the kingdom of glory and predestined as vessels of wrath and disgrace, prepared for punishment.
chunks.notes: Echoes Romans 9:22-23 regarding vessels of wrath and vessels of mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cum scriptum sit: Pater, dilexisti eos ante mundi constitutionem; videntur necessitate salvari, qui ante mundi exordium sunt dilecti.
chunks.english: Student: Since it is written in the Gospel of John, chapter seventeen, verse twenty-four, 'Father, you loved them before the foundation of the world,' those who were loved before the world's beginning seem to be saved by necessity.
chunks.notes: The citation is from John 17:24 in Jesus's high priestly prayer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Deo nihil est futurum: sed omnia sunt ei praesentia, quae jam sunt, et quae futura sunt. Omnes ergo futuri semper in Dei praesentia fuerunt, et ideo ante mundi constitutionem sunt dilecti: sed suo tempore pro meritis ad gloriam electi.
chunks.english: Master: Nothing is future to God. Rather, all things are present to him, both what already exists and what will exist. Therefore all future people were always present in God's sight, and for this reason they were loved before the foundation of the world. But they were chosen for glory in their own time according to their merits.
chunks.notes: This reflects the classical theological concept of God's eternal present.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quos ergo Deus ante mundi constitutionem praedestinando elegit, hos non necessitas, sed proprium meritum ad gloriam traxit. Quibus ipse in fine mundi dicturus erit: Venite, benedicti Patris mei, percipite regnum quod vobis paratum est ab origine mundi.
chunks.english: Therefore those whom God chose by predestining them before the foundation of the world were drawn to glory not by necessity but by their own merit. To them he will say at the end of the world, as recorded in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse thirty-four: 'Come, blessed of my Father, receive the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world.'
chunks.notes: Citation is from Matthew 25:34 in the parable of the sheep and goats.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quod hoc regnum pro meritis eis detur, divina vox ostendit, quae mox eorum bona replicando exsequitur.
chunks.english: And that this kingdom is given to them according to their merits is shown by the divine voice, which immediately follows by recounting their good deeds.
chunks.notes: Refers to Matthew 25:35-36 where Christ enumerates the corporal works of mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Remoto ergo repagulo Praedestinationis, eia jam ingredere silvam exspectatae disputationis. Ut quid sit liberum arbitrium. Et quomodo ei non repugnet Praedestinatio vel Praescientia. Et qualiter concordet Gratiae, resera, et quaecunque inde capita surgentia ense rationis reseca.
chunks.english: Student: Now that the barrier of predestination has been removed, come, enter the forest of the expected discussion. Unlock what free will is, and how predestination and foreknowledge do not contradict it, and how it harmonizes with grace. Cut down with the sword of reason whatever branches spring up from this.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Dic tu mihi, quid sit liberum arbitrium, et quid arbitreris.
chunks.english: Master: You tell me what free will is and what you think about it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Mihi videtur, liberum arbitrium dici libertas bonum vel malum eligendi.
chunks.english: Student: It seems to me that free will is called the freedom to choose good or evil.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Haec definitio licet plerisque placeat, vereor ne perspicax ratio eam abnuat.
chunks.english: Master: Although this definition pleases most people, I fear that penetrating reason rejects it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Electio namque non nisi de duabus vel pluribus rebus dicitur; ubi optio eligendi de multis proponitur. Angelo autem vel homini, quibus solis datum est liberum arbitrium, non nisi sola justitia proponebatur, qua servata, aeterna beatitudine fruerentur.
chunks.english: For choice is only spoken of regarding two or more things, where the option of choosing among many is proposed. But to the angel and to the human being, to whom alone free will is given, only justice alone was proposed, by keeping which they would enjoy eternal blessedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Igitur definitio non videtur conveniens libero arbitrio.
chunks.english: Therefore the definition does not seem fitting for free will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid si dicatur: quod mors et vita homini proposita sint; et ipse mortem elegerit?
chunks.english: Student: What if it is said that death and life were proposed to the human being, and he chose death?
chunks.notes: Allusion to Deuteronomy 30:19 and Sirach 15:17.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Non hoc est verum: non enim mortem elegit, sed deceptus altiorem scientiam concupivit.
chunks.english: Master: This is not true. He did not choose death, but being deceived, he desired higher knowledge.
chunks.notes: Reference to Genesis 3 and the temptation narrative.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid si dicatur: quod bonum a Deo proponatur; malum autem a diabolo suadeatur, et in libero arbitrio hominis sit, quid eligere velit?
chunks.english: Student: What if it is said that good is proposed by God, evil is urged by the devil, and it is in the human being's free will to choose what he wishes?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. De homine hoc utrumque dici; de Angelo non potest, cum nemo fuerit, qui ei aliquid mali persuaserit. Porro inter bonum et malum nihil est medium. Sed si homo in aliquo medio constitutus videretur, tunc fortassis, recte, libertas bonum vel malum eligendi ei inesse diceretur. Sed cum in bono tantum sit constitutus, et nihil aliud sit mali electio, quam quaedam animi in bono defectio, cum videlicet deserit justitiam, quam servare debuit, et ad injustitiam declinat, quam devitandam non ignorat.
chunks.english: Master: Both of these can be said about the human being, but not about the angel, since there was no one who persuaded him of anything evil. Moreover, there is no middle ground between good and evil. But if the human being seemed to be placed in some middle position, then perhaps rightly the freedom to choose good or evil would be said to exist in him. But since he is established only in the good, and the choice of evil is nothing other than a certain falling away of the soul from the good—namely when it abandons the justice it ought to preserve and declines toward injustice, which it knows it should avoid.
chunks.notes: This reflects Augustinian theology of evil as privation rather than positive reality.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Sed quae sit ejus definitio magnopere exspecto.
chunks.english: Student: But I greatly await what its definition is.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Arbitrium est propriae voluntatis judicium quid velit, quidque nolit. Hoc liberum dicitur: quia non est necessarium, sed quibus inest ratio, inest etiam volendi nolendique libertas.
chunks.english: Master: Free judgment is the determination of one's own will regarding what it wishes and what it does not wish. This is called free because it is not necessary, but in those who have reason, there also exists the freedom of willing and not willing.
chunks.notes: This definition closely follows Anselm of Canterbury.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia liberum arbitrium pro sola justitia servanda (in qua salus animae consistit) datur, ejus definitio sic congrua aestimatur.
chunks.english: And because free will is given solely for preserving justice, in which the salvation of the soul consists, its definition is considered fitting in this way.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Libertas arbitrii est potestas servandi rectitudinem voluntatis, propter ipsam rectitudinem. Recta quippe voluntas est, velle quod vult Deus. Deus autem vult, ut rationalis creatura ei non coacte, sed sponte subdita sit, ut ei bene sit; quando et hoc ipsa vult, tunc voluntas ejus recta est.
chunks.english: The freedom of choice is the power to preserve the rightness of the will for the sake of that rightness itself. For the right will is to will what God wills. God wills that the rational creature be subject to him not by compulsion but voluntarily, so that it may be well with the creature. When the creature also wills this, then its will is right.
chunks.notes: This is a direct adaptation of Anselm's definition from De Libertate Arbitrii.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad hanc rectitudinem servandam est libera, nulla necessitate constricta: rectitudo autem voluntatis est justitia. Si ergo justitiam non propter aliud quam propter ipsam justitiam diligit Deum (qui est justitia, et summa beatitudo) praemium habebit.
chunks.english: The will is free to preserve this rightness, constrained by no necessity. The rightness of the will is justice. Therefore if it loves God, who is justice and supreme blessedness, not for any other reason than for the sake of justice itself, it will have its reward.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Igitur convenientissima definitio liberi arbitrii est: potestas servandi rectitudinem voluntatis propter ipsam rectitudinem, per quam jure possidet aeternam beatitudinem.
chunks.english: Therefore the most fitting definition of free will is this: the power to preserve the rightness of the will for the sake of that rightness itself, through which one rightfully possesses eternal blessedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Sed quomodo, nunc habet liberum arbitrium ad servandam justitiam; cum non habeat justitiam?
chunks.english: Student: But how does one now have free will for preserving justice when one does not have justice?
chunks.notes: This addresses the problem of fallen humanity lacking original justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Sicut aliquis habet visum, ad videndum solem, cum ipse absit, quem utique videre potest si adsit: ita homo habet naturaliter liberum arbitrium, ad servandam justitiam, etiamsi ipsa desit, quam servare potest si adsit.
chunks.english: Master: Just as someone has sight for seeing the sun when it is absent, which he certainly can see if it is present, so the human being naturally has free will for preserving justice, even if it is absent, which he can preserve if it is present.
chunks.notes: Classic Anselmian analogy distinguishing capacity from actuality.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hanc autem non habitam, per se habere non potest: quia nullus (excepto Deo) habet quod non accepit, quae si ei datur, gratia ejus est, qui ait: Sine me nihil potestis facere. Acceptam autem justitiam non deserere, liberi arbitrii est. Hanc autem servare, meritum est.
chunks.english: But this justice which he does not possess, he cannot possess by himself, because no one except God has what he has not received, as stated in Paul's first letter to the Corinthians, chapter four, verse seven. If it is given to him, it is the grace of the one who says in the Gospel of John, chapter fifteen, verse five: 'Without me you can do nothing.' Not to abandon the justice one has received is a matter of free will. To preserve this justice is merit.
chunks.notes: Combines 1 Corinthians 4:7 and John 15:5.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ideo non volentis est: subaudi quod vult: nec currentis, subaudi quod currit: sed miserentis Dei: qui (nullis praecedentibus meritis, sed gratis) gratia eum praevenit, dando, ut justus sit: et subsequitur, ut in stadio justitiae currat, quatenus bravium vitae suscipiat, aut percipiat.
chunks.english: Therefore, as stated in Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter nine, verse sixteen, it does not depend on the one who wills—understand, what he wills—nor on the one who runs—understand, what he runs—but on God who shows mercy. God's grace goes before him without any preceding merits, but freely, by giving him the ability to be just, and follows him so that he may run in the stadium of justice, so that he may receive or obtain the prize of life.
chunks.notes: Romans 9:16 with metaphor from 1 Corinthians 9:24 regarding the race.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicuti, si egens nudus a divite vestiatur: non est ipsius, quod vestitur, sed ejus qui vestem largitur. In suo autem arbitrio est, utrum vestem portet, aut deponat. Sic est de gratia praeveniente, libero arbitrio comitante.
chunks.english: Just as if a poor naked person is clothed by a rich person, it is not his doing that he is clothed, but the doing of the one who gives the clothing. But it is in his own choice whether he wears the garment or takes it off. So it is with prevenient grace and accompanying free will.
chunks.notes: Illustrates the cooperation of grace and free will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Cum Apostolus dicat: Antequam vel bonum vel malum scirent, dictum est: Jacob dilexi, Esau autem odio habui: videntur quidam non secundum merita, sed secundum propositum Dei salvari, vel damnari: aut Deus videtur quorumdam facta punire, antequam perpetrentur. Nam ob quod meritum Jacob diligi meruit, qui nondum bonum facere scivit? Ob quem etiam reatum Esau odio habitus, qui necdum aliquid mali operatus est?
chunks.english: Student: Since the Apostle says in his letter to the Romans, chapter nine, verses eleven through thirteen: 'Before they were born or had done anything good or evil, so that God's purpose according to election might stand, not from works but from the one who calls, it was said, The older will serve the younger, as it is written, Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated'—some people seem to be saved or damned not according to merit but according to God's purpose, or God seems to punish some people's deeds before they commit them. For on account of what merit did Jacob deserve to be loved, when he did not yet know how to do good? And for what guilt was Esau held in hatred, when he had not yet done anything evil?
chunks.notes: Romans 9:11-13, one of the most difficult passages regarding predestination.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Sicut superius dictum est: Deo nihil est futurum. Sacra Scriptura testatur Jacob simplicem, Esau profanum. Et quod ipsi suo tempore per liberum arbitrium erant futuri, hoc, antequam nati essent, erant in conspectu Domini.
chunks.english: Master: As was said earlier, nothing is future to God. Sacred Scripture testifies that Jacob was upright and Esau profane. And what they were going to become in their own time through free will, this they already were in the Lord's sight before they were born.
chunks.notes: References Genesis 25:27 describing Jacob as 'a quiet man' and Hebrews 12:16 calling Esau 'profane' or 'godless.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ergo quia Jacob in conspectu Domini simplex fuit, ideo Dominus eum dilexit: quia vero Esau profanus erat, ideo eum odio habuit.
chunks.english: Therefore because Jacob was upright in the Lord's sight, the Lord loved him. But because Esau was profane, he held him in hatred.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dilexi autem praeteritum tempus, propter aeternitatem posuit: quia sicut praeteritum, semper est praeteritum, ita immutabile est omne, quod aeternitas praevidet fiendum.
chunks.english: He used the past tense 'I loved' because of eternity, for just as the past is always past, so everything that eternity foresees as going to happen is immutable.
chunks.notes: Explains why God speaks in past tense about future contingents from his eternal perspective.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Porro quia injustitia non est apud Deum, nullum sine praecedente injustitia punit, sed multis perpetrata peccata misericorditer ignoscit.
chunks.english: Moreover, because there is no injustice with God, as Romans chapter nine, verse fourteen states, he punishes no one without preceding injustice, but mercifully forgives many sins that have been committed.
chunks.notes: Romans 9:14.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Igitur licet necesse sit, omne fieri, quod Deus fieri proposuit, praescivit, praedestinavit: tamen propositum, praescientia, praedestinatio, nullam vim necessitatis alicui inferunt; quin quisque libero arbitrio facit quod vult.
chunks.english: Therefore, although it is necessary that everything happen which God has purposed, foreknown, and predestined to happen, nevertheless purpose, foreknowledge, and predestination impose no force of necessity on anyone. Rather, each person does what he wills by free will.
chunks.notes: Key thesis reconciling divine sovereignty and human freedom.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et qui per liberum arbitrium declinant a malo et faciunt bonum, hi sunt populus et oves pascuae ejus; et his praeparavit pascua vitae, et hos elegit in Christo ante mundi constitutionem.
chunks.english: And those who through free will turn away from evil and do good, these are his people and the sheep of his pasture, as stated in Psalm seventy-eight, verse thirteen. For these he prepared the pastures of life, and these he chose in Christ before the foundation of the world, as Ephesians chapter one, verse four declares.
chunks.notes: Combines Psalm 78:13 (or 79:13 in Septuagint numbering) and Ephesians 1:4.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vid. D. Anselmum et divers. locis illius libri. ] Qui autem a libertate deficiunt, et nulla necessitate cogente, sed sponte se servituti subjiciunt, dum servi peccati effecti (Joan. VIII, 34) , daemonibus, qui vitiis praestant, jugiter serviunt; hi sunt populus Pharaonis, et non sunt ex ovibus Domini, sed sunt oves in inferno ponendae, quas mors depascet (Psal. XLVIII, 15) . Hi tales, tot doctrinis auditis, tot signis visis, tot plagis perpessis, incorrigibiles sunt, et eos Dominus abjicit, cum eos juste in injustitia derelinquit.
chunks.english: See Anselm and various places in his book. But those who abandon their freedom, and with no necessity compelling them, but willingly submit themselves to slavery, having become servants of sin, as the Gospel of John, chapter eight, verse thirty-four states, continually serve the demons who preside over vices. These are the people of Pharaoh, and they are not among the sheep of the Lord, but are sheep to be placed in hell, whom death will devour, as Psalm forty-eight, verse fifteen declares. Such people, having heard so many teachings, seen so many signs, suffered so many plagues, remain incorrigible, and the Lord rejects them when he justly abandons them in their injustice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Itaque Praedestinatio et Praescientia Dei immutabiles et fixae manent: quia omnia, quae fienda praescit, et praedestinat Deus, indeclinabiliter fient. [Col. 1202B] Gratia autem et liberum arbitrium, salutem hominis concordi foedere operatur: homo vero a libertate sola voluntate deficiens, ad peccatum inclinatur.
chunks.english: Therefore God's Predestination and Foreknowledge remain unchangeable and fixed, because all things that God foreknows and predestines to happen will happen without deviation. But grace and free will work together in harmonious covenant for the salvation of humanity. Yet man, failing in his freedom by his will alone, inclines toward sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quia nodum liberi arbitrii per adjutricem gratiam eleganter enodasti: rogant, insinues, si diabolus ad beatitudinem conditus sit.
chunks.english: Student: Since you have elegantly untied the knot of free will through the help of grace, they ask that you explain whether the devil was created for blessedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Constat quod rationalis creatura ad beatitudinem sit condita: Diabolus autem rationalis creatura est: igitur ad beatitudinem est conditus. Scriptum quippe de illo est: plenus sapientia et perfectus decore, in deliciis paradisi Dei fuisti (Ezech. XXVIII, 12, 13) .
chunks.english: Master: It is established that every rational creature was created for blessedness. But the devil is a rational creature; therefore he was created for blessedness. For it is written of him in the book of Ezekiel, chapter twenty-eight, verses twelve and thirteen: You were full of wisdom and perfect in beauty, you were in the delights of God's paradise.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et quid est, quod de eo Dominus dicit: [Col. 1202C] quod in veritate non stetit (Joan. VIII, 44) .
chunks.english: Student: And what about what the Lord says of him, that he did not stand in the truth, as the Gospel of John, chapter eight, verse forty-four records?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Id est, in rectitudine voluntatis non permansit: justitiam enim sponte deseruit. Et ideo juste beatitudinem amisit.
chunks.english: Master: That is, he did not remain in the rightness of his will. For he willingly abandoned justice, and therefore justly lost his blessedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid est Beatitudo?
chunks.english: Student: What is blessedness?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Omnium bonorum sufficientia, sine omni indigentia.
chunks.english: Master: The sufficiency of all good things, without any need whatsoever.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur non fecit eum talem, ut hanc amittere non posset?
chunks.english: Student: Why didn't God make him such that he could not lose this?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Si talem eum fecisset, tunc necessitati ut bestia subjacuisset. Fecit autem cum justum, beatum, liberum. Justum, ut vellet, quod Deus voluit: beatum, ut semper Deo frueretur: liberum, ut sponte justitiam vellet, et hoc pro merito jure semper beatus foret.
chunks.english: Master: If God had made him that way, he would have been subjected to necessity like a beast. But God made him just, blessed, and free. Just, so that he would want what God wanted; blessed, so that he would always enjoy God; free, so that he would voluntarily want justice, and by this merit would rightfully always be blessed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: [Col. 1202D] DISC. Et cur ab hac gloria cecidit diabolus?
chunks.english: Student: And why did the devil fall from this glory?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quia hoc appetiit, quod non debuit, et quod Deus noluit, et similis Altissimo esse voluit (Isa. XIV, 14) .
chunks.english: Master: Because he desired what he should not have, and what God did not will, and he wanted to be like the Most High, as Isaiah chapter fourteen, verse fourteen states.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quomodo similis?
chunks.english: Student: In what way similar?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Deus dici voluit; et ut Deus ab angelis adorari voluit.
chunks.english: Master: He wanted to be called God, and he wanted to be worshiped by the angels as God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: [Col. 1203A] DISC. Cum sit illocalis, ut puta incorporeus ( Angelus ): unde et quomodo cecidit?
chunks.english: Student: Since he is not located in a place, being incorporeal as an angel, from where and how did he fall?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Sicut aliquis in luce solis stans, si subito visum amitteret, diceretur in tenebras incidisse, cum locum non mutaverit, praesertim cum in nullo fuerit.
chunks.english: Master: Just as someone standing in the light of the sun, if he suddenly lost his sight, would be said to have fallen into darkness, even though he has not changed location, especially since he was not in any particular place.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cum Deus per omnia beatus, et sibi semper sufficiens fuerit, quae causa exstitit, quod aliquid creare voluit?
chunks.english: Student: Since God was completely blessed in all things and always sufficient to himself, what was the reason that he wanted to create anything?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Propter bonitatem suam creavit omnia, ut essent, in quos effueret suae bonitatis abundantia Prov. XVI, 4. Universa propter semetipsum operatus est Dominus, impium quoque ad diem malum. ] : Omnis namque creatura aliquo modo fruitur bonitate creatoris Dei. Quaedam, quod sunt; quaedam, quod vivunt; quaedam, quod sentiunt; quaedam, [Col. 1203B] quod intelligunt. Singula ergo sunt bona; universa considerata valde bona Eccli. XXXIX, 21. Opera Domini universa bona valde. ] .
chunks.english: Master: Because of his goodness he created all things, so that they might exist, into which the abundance of his goodness might flow, as the book of Proverbs, chapter sixteen, verse four declares: The Lord has made all things for himself, even the wicked for the day of evil. For every creature in some way enjoys the goodness of God the Creator. Some in that they exist; some in that they live; some in that they feel; some in that they understand. Therefore each thing is good, and all things considered together are very good, as Ecclesiasticus chapter thirty-nine, verse twenty-one says: All the works of the Lord are very good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Angelicam autem naturam et humanam, ad se videndum condidit; quae ut aliquod proprium meritum apud eum haberent, quod remunerare deberet; justitiam eis dedit. Ut autem plenum gaudium haberent, liberum arbitrium addidit, quo non coacti, sed liberi, justitiam datam servarent; et sic semper cum eo bene regnarent.
chunks.english: But he created angelic and human nature to see him. And so that they might have some proper merit with him which he ought to reward, he gave them justice. And so that they might have full joy, he added free will, by which they would keep the justice given to them not by compulsion but freely, and thus would always reign well with him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et si ad beatitudinem conditi sunt, cur tot pereunt?
chunks.english: Student: And if they were created for blessedness, why do so many perish?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quia per liberum arbitrium a bono deficiunt, et peccato se subjiciunt; juste gloria beatitudinis privantur, et poenis mancipantur.
chunks.english: Master: Because through free will they fall away from the good and submit themselves to sin. They are justly deprived of the glory of blessedness and handed over to punishments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et quomodo verum est quod Deus vult [Col. 1203C] omnes salvos fieri? (I Tim. II, 4.)
chunks.english: Student: And how is it true that God wills all people to be saved, as Paul's first letter to Timothy, chapter two, verse four states?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Deus condidit rationalem creaturam ad gloriam; sed angeli et homines, rationales creaturae sunt: ergo omnes ad gloriam creati sunt. Quod autem ad gloriam eos condidit, hoc volens aut nolens fecit.
chunks.english: Master: God created rational creatures for glory. But angels and humans are rational creatures; therefore all were created for glory. And that he created them for glory, he did this either willingly or unwillingly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Nolens non potuit.
chunks.english: Student: He could not have done so unwillingly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Volens ergo fecit.
chunks.english: Master: Therefore he did it willingly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Hoc sequitur.
chunks.english: Student: That follows.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quod autem semel Deus vult, hoc semper vult. Est enim voluntas ejus immutabilis. Igitur semper omnes vult salvos fieri.
chunks.english: Master: And what God wills once, he always wills. For his will is unchangeable. Therefore he always wills all to be saved.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et si vult, cur omnes non salvantur? Voluntati enim ejus quis resistit? (Rom. IX, 19.)
chunks.english: Student: And if he wills this, why are not all saved? For who can resist his will, as Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter nine, verse nineteen asks?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: [Col. 1203D] MAG. Perditio pereuntium non procedit ex voluntate Dei, quia non vult mortem peccatoris (Ezech. XVIII, 32) , et qui mortem non fecit, nec laetatur in perditione morientium Sap. I, 13. Nec laetatur in perditione vivorum. ] ; sed ex libero arbitrio, quo sponte justitiam, quae est pactum beatitudinis, deserunt: qua deserta, beatitudinem amittunt; et sic semper miseri erunt.
chunks.english: Master: The perdition of those who perish does not proceed from the will of God, because he does not will the death of the sinner, as Ezekiel chapter eighteen, verse thirty-two declares, and he who did not make death does not rejoice in the perdition of the dying, as the book of Wisdom chapter one, verse thirteen says: Nor does he rejoice in the perdition of the living. Rather it comes from free will, by which they willingly abandon justice, which is the covenant of blessedness. Having abandoned this, they lose blessedness, and thus will always be wretched.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quomodo ex libero arbirio?
chunks.english: Student: How does this come from free will?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Angelus nulla praedestinatione, nulla necessitate trahente; sed sola libera voluntate, justitiam deseruit dum Deo similis esse voluit (Isa. XIV, 14) . Et quia propria voluntate, vinculum beatitudinis, [Col. 1204A] quod erat justitia, abrupit; juste amissa beatitudine in miseriam incidit, de qua, justitia dictante, nunquam eripi meruit.
chunks.english: Master: The angel, with no predestination and no necessity drawing him, but by free will alone, abandoned justice when he wanted to be like God, as Isaiah chapter fourteen, verse fourteen recounts. And because by his own will he broke the bond of blessedness, which was justice, he justly fell into misery having lost blessedness, from which, as justice dictates, he never merited to be rescued.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc ratio justitiae exegit: etenim qui nullo impellente corruit, juste a nullo levari debuit.
chunks.english: The principle of justice demanded this. For he who fell with no one pushing him ought justly to be lifted by no one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid est de homine?
chunks.english: Student: What about man?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Homo similiter nulla vi coactus, nulla necessitate impulsus, sed libera voluntate justitiam deseruit, et injustitiae se subdidit, dum sicut Deus esse concupivit.
chunks.english: Master: Man similarly, forced by no power, compelled by no necessity, but by free will abandoned justice and submitted himself to injustice when he desired to be like God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur hic lapsus erigi meruit?
chunks.english: Student: Why did this fallen one merit to be raised up?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hujus reparatio, gratia fuit; sed et ratio justitiae hoc fieri poposcit: etenim qui alio impellente cecidit, juste ab alio levari debuit.
chunks.english: Master: His restoration was grace, but the principle of justice also demanded that this happen. For he who fell because another pushed him ought justly to be lifted by another.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Deum, ut arbitror, eis liberum arbitrium [Col. 1204B] dare non decuit, per quod eos peccaturos praescivit.
chunks.english: Student: I think it was not fitting for God to give them free will, through which he foreknew they would sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hoc est tale, quale dicas: Scientiam eis dare non oportuit, qua eos abuti praescivit. Si scientiam eis non dedisset, quid a brutis animalibus distarent? Si liberum arbitrium non dedisset: necessitati per omnia, ut bestiae, subjacerent, nec ullum meritum apud Deum haberent.
chunks.english: Master: That's like saying: He ought not to have given them knowledge, which he foreknew they would abuse. If he had not given them knowledge, how would they differ from brute animals? If he had not given them free will, they would be subjected to necessity in all things like beasts, and would have no merit before God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Oportuit ergo ut eis scientiam daret, qua Creatorem agnoscerent. Liberum etiam arbitrium adderetur, quo libere justitiam servarent, per quam semper beati forent.
chunks.english: Therefore it was necessary that he give them knowledge by which they would recognize the Creator, and that free will also be added, by which they would freely keep justice, through which they would always be blessed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Si Deus omnia praescit; tunc utique eos male acturos, et per hoc aeternum supplicium passuros praescivit: cur ergo voluit eos creare, qui tot et [Col. 1204C] tanta flagitia et facinora erant facturi; et pro his aeternas poenas laturi.
chunks.english: Student: If God foreknows all things, then surely he foreknew that they would act wickedly and through this would suffer eternal punishment. Why then did he want to create those who were going to commit so many and such great crimes and misdeeds, and for these would bear eternal punishments?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Deus omnia praescivit; et hoc aeternitati ejus congruit, qui nihil futurum, quasi fiendum; nec aliquid praeteritum, quasi transactum novit; sed omnia fienda et transacta, immutabili intuitu praesentia inspicit.
chunks.english: Master: God foreknew all things, and this befits his eternity. He knows nothing as future, as if it were yet to be done, nor anything as past, as if it were finished. Rather, he beholds all things to be done and all things done as present with unchangeable vision.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod autem angelum vel hominem creavit, quem utrumque male acturum praescivit: hoc ratio justitiae et potentiae poposcit. Potentiae, quia praescivit, quod illorum malitia nihil sibi penitus nocere potuit, et quanta bona ipse de malis eorum in universitate facere disposuit. Justitiae autem, quia justum erat, ut omne id Deus faceret, quod ad aliquid in universitate utile [Col. 1204D] foret. Quam utiles autem, et quam necessarii in Dei republica sint daemones vel mali homines, non ignorant sapientes.
chunks.english: But that he created the angel or man, each of whom he foreknew would act wickedly, this the principle of justice and power demanded. Power, because he foreknew that their malice could utterly harm him not at all, and how many good things he planned to make from their evils in the universe. Justice, because it was just that God should do everything that would be useful for something in the universe. And the wise do not fail to see how useful and how necessary demons or evil men are in God's commonwealth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Creatis ergo justitiam dedit, quam eos minime servaturos praescivit; imo eos justos condidit, quia hoc bonitati ejus congruit, ut nihil nisi justum et rectum faceret. Quibus etiam liberum arbitrium dedit, id est suae voluntatis compotes fecit; quia hoc suae perfectioni convenit, ut nihil nisi perfectum absolveret: perfecti enim non essent, si liberi non essent. Et si liberi non essent, necessitati, ut bestiae, subjacerent. Ergo [Col. 1205A] totum fecit, ut voluit, et ut facere debuit.
chunks.english: Therefore he gave justice to those he created, which he foreknew they would not keep at all. Indeed, he created them just, because it befits his goodness to do nothing except what is just and right. To them also he gave free will, that is, he made them masters of their own will, because it befits his perfection to accomplish nothing except what is perfect. For they would not be perfect if they were not free. And if they were not free, they would be subjected to necessity like beasts. Therefore he did everything as he willed and as he ought to have done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur eos Deus a malo non prohibuit cum posset? si voluit et non potuit, cur impotens fuit; si autem potuit et noluit, cur eos peccare, et consequenter pati poenas voluit? Et cum peccare permisit, peccatis utique consensit. Si enim aliquis princeps praescit, quod sibi quilibet subjectus perimere velit; et non prohibet dum possit, utique homicidis consentit, dum illud fieri permittit. Si autem volens permittit, impotentem se ostendit.
chunks.english: Student: Why did God not prohibit them from evil when he could have? If he willed it and could not, why was he powerless? But if he could and would not, why did he will them to sin and consequently to suffer punishments? And since he permitted them to sin, he surely consented to their sins. For if any prince foreknows that some subject wants to kill him, and does not prohibit it when he can, he surely consents to the homicide when he permits it to happen. But if he permits it willingly, he shows himself powerless.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quod eos Deus a malo non prohibuit, hoc ratio immutabilitatis vetuit. Deus namque condidit eos ad plenum gaudium, plenum autem gaudium non haberent, si liberi non essent. [Col. 1205B] Ut ergo plenum gaudium haberent, dedit eis liberum arbitrium; imo creavit eos liberos, id est suae voluntatis compotes, ut libere in laetitia delectarentur; et non necessitate coacti vel violentia compulsi, gaudio fruerentur.
chunks.english: Master: That God did not prohibit them from evil, this the principle of immutability forbade. For God created them for full joy, but they would not have full joy if they were not free. Therefore, so that they might have full joy, he gave them free will. Indeed, he created them free, that is, masters of their own will, so that they might freely delight in happiness, and not enjoy happiness forced by necessity or compelled by violence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Libertatem autem quam semel eis dedit, immutabilis Deus auferre non debuit, nec etiam potuit. Si enim abstulisset, tunc nollet eos justitiam velle, quod impossibile est; voluit enim eos velle justitiam. Nihil enim aliud est peccare quam justitiam nolle. Et ideo malum vel peccatum nihil est per substantiam, sicut injustitia. Injustitia autem nihil aliud est quam ubi non est justitia. Sicut silentium, ubi non est vox, sicut tenebrae ubi non est lux. Malum itaque facere non est aliud quam a justa voluntate [Col. 1205C] deficere. Libertatem autem quodammodo Deus eis abstulisset, si ab eo cohibiti, peccare non possent. Et si coacti justitiam servassent, meritum justitiae non haberent.
chunks.english: But the freedom which he once gave them, the immutable God ought not to have taken away, nor could he have. For if he had taken it away, then he would not want them to want justice, which is impossible, since he willed them to want justice. For to sin is nothing other than to not want justice. And therefore evil or sin is nothing by substance, just like injustice. But injustice is nothing other than where justice is not, just as silence is where there is no voice, just as darkness is where there is no light. Therefore to do evil is nothing other than to fail from a just will. And God would in some way have taken away their freedom if, restrained by him, they could not sin. And if they had kept justice by compulsion, they would not have had the merit of justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid? nonne boni angeli peccare non possunt?
chunks.english: Student: What? Can't the good angels sin?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hoc non est ex impotentiae necessitate, sed ex magnae potentiae procedit voluntate; magna quippe est potentia semper velle, quod quis deberet, et decet, verbi gratia semper sanum et sapientem esse. Maxima vero impotentia, imo dementia, velle quod non debet nec decet, ut se velle interimere. Etenim aliis angelis ad injustitiam declinantibus, ipsi boni angeli justitiam libera voluntate servaverunt: et pro hoc merito, mox in hac voluntate sic [Col. 1205D] confirmati sunt, ut ultra malum nolint. Et ideo dicitur: Quod non possunt.
chunks.english: Master: This is not from the necessity of impotence, but proceeds from the will of great power. For it is great power to always want what one ought and what befits, for example to always be healthy and wise. But it is maximum impotence, indeed madness, to want what one ought not and what does not befit, such as wanting to kill oneself. For when other angels declined toward injustice, the good angels themselves kept justice by free will, and for this merit they were immediately so confirmed in this will that they no longer want evil. And therefore it is said that they cannot.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Sed, ut dictum est, Deus videtur peccato eorum consensisse, quos peccare permisit; vel potius ad poenas creasse, quos a peccato non prohibuit.
chunks.english: Student: But as was said, God seems to have consented to their sin when he permitted them to sin, or rather to have created them for punishments when he did not prohibit them from sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Veritas consulta longe aliud ostendit. Quamvis viae Domini a viis hominum elongentur, quantum coeli a terra exaltantur Isa. LV, 8. Non enim cogitationes meae cogitationes vestrae; neque viae vestrae, viae meae, dicit Dominus. [Col. 1206D] Quia sicut exaltantur coeli a terra, sic exaltatae sunt viae meae a viis vestris etc.] ; tamen non multum a cogitationibus nostris haec res discrepat, quam nobis perspecta ratio sic elucidat.
chunks.english: Master: When truth is consulted, it shows something quite different. Although the ways of the Lord are as far removed from the ways of men as the heavens are exalted above the earth, as Isaiah chapter fifty-five, verse eight declares—For my thoughts are not your thoughts, nor are your ways my ways, says the Lord. Because as the heavens are exalted above the earth, so are my ways exalted above your ways—nevertheless this matter does not differ greatly from our thoughts, which clear reasoning thus illuminates for us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non semper sequitur, ut qui aliquid fieri permittit, [Col. 1206A] in fiendo consentiat, si prohibere possit. Nam cum aliquis in agro suo spinas nasci permittit, cum prohibere possit; nec tamen consentit, neque ad sepis munimentum, neque ad ignium nutrimentum crescere sinit, cum ad utrumque aptae sint; sic Deus, cum eos peccare permisit, peccato tamen non consensit, nec ad poenas creavit; quos tamen post peccata poenis mancipavit, sed libera voluntate, sibi, qui est summa beatitudo, inhaerentes, gloria et honore coronavit (Psal. VIII, 6) : libera nihilominus voluntate, a se qui est aeterna justitia, recedentes congruis suppliciis multavit; et illorum asperitate, ut spinis sepem, istorum gloriam firmat, et illorum concrematione universum opus suum illustravit.
chunks.english: It does not always follow that whoever permits something to happen consents to its happening, if he could prohibit it. For when someone permits thorns to grow in his field, though he could prohibit it, he nevertheless does not consent, nor does he allow them to grow for the fortification of a hedge or for fuel for fires, though they are suitable for both. So God, when he permitted them to sin, nevertheless did not consent to sin, nor did he create them for punishments. However, after their sins he handed them over to punishments. But those who by free will cling to him who is supreme blessedness, he crowned with glory and honor, as Psalm eight, verse six declares. But those who by free will depart from him who is eternal justice, he punished with fitting torments. And by their harshness, as with thorns for a hedge, he strengthens the glory of the former, and by their burning he illuminated his entire work.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur illos Deus creavit, quos justitiam minime [Col. 1206B] servaturos, et ideo gloriam amissuros praescivit, et non illos solummodo, quos pro servanda justitia praescivit permansuros in gloria?
chunks.english: Student: Why did God create those whom he foreknew would not keep justice at all and therefore would lose glory, and not only those whom he foreknew would remain in glory for keeping justice?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hoc est tale, quale causeris, cur Deus noctem permiserit esse, cum sit tenebrosa; dies autem lucidus, quasi sit inutilis, et non necessaria: cum potius ipsa nox sit requies laborantium et reparatio virium. Sic bonum per malum commendatur. Justi enim injustis comparati gloriosiores apparent, sicut dies nocti collatus, gratiosior claret. Ratio ergo boni poscebat, ut Deus malum esse permitteret, per quod reciprocis modulis, per contrarietates suas in omnibus consonaret. Quamvis enim malum nihil sit per substantiam; et ideo aeternae Dei dispositioni nullam ingerat dissonantiam; substantia [Col. 1206C] tamen per idipsum quod est, facit omnes substantias bonas apparere; et innotescit quam bonum sit summo bono inhaerere.
chunks.english: Master: This is like complaining why God permitted night to exist, since it is dark, as if day were useless and unnecessary, when rather night itself is the rest of laborers and the restoration of strength. So good is commended through evil. For the just compared to the unjust appear more glorious, just as day compared to night shines more graciously. Therefore the principle of good demanded that God permit evil to exist, through which, by reciprocal measures, through its contraries it might harmonize in all things. For although evil is nothing by substance, and therefore brings no dissonance to God's eternal disposition, nevertheless substance through the very fact that it exists makes all substances appear good, and it becomes known how good it is to cling to the supreme good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Valde mirum de bonitate Dei videtur: quod aliquid creare voluit, quod aeternis doloribus, aternisque cruciatibus subderetur, cum videretur multo melius, aliquam rem non subsistere, quam misere subsistere aut ut lapidem insensibilem esse, quam aeternos dolores tolerare.
chunks.english: Student: It seems very amazing about the goodness of God that he wanted to create something that would be subjected to eternal pains and eternal torments, when it would seem much better for something not to exist than to exist miserably, or to be an insensible stone than to endure eternal pains.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Sapienti Deo melius visum est, esse quod utrumque est, quam nullo modo esse. Et ideo fecit esse, quod est. Quod autem non fecit omnia aequalia, sed disparia, per hoc cognoscitur et ineffabilis sapientia artificis, et inaestimabilis adornata plenitudo operis. [Col. 1206D] Et quamvis Deus poenas fecerit, nihil tamen ad poenas creavit: alioquin praestantior insensibilis creatura, quam sensibilis esset: quae sine fine, dolorem poenae sentiret.
chunks.english: Master: It seemed better to wise God that what is both exist, than not to exist at all. And therefore he made to exist what exists. But that he did not make all things equal, but unequal, through this both the ineffable wisdom of the craftsman is known, and the inestimably adorned fullness of the work. And although God made punishments, he nevertheless created nothing for punishments. Otherwise insensible creation would be superior to sensible creation, which would feel the pain of punishment without end.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Rationalis creatura porro dum sponte justitiam habitam abjecit, tunc propositam gloriam amisit; sicque miseriam incidit. Est autem miseria, inde dolere quod amisit, nec posse habere quod cupit, et his in quibus est, affligi; nec his posse exui. Poenis autem cruciari, non est aliud, quam sibi contrarium contra voluntatem pati.
chunks.english: Furthermore, when rational creation willingly cast off the justice it possessed, then it lost the glory set before it, and thus fell into misery. Misery is to grieve over what was lost, and not to be able to have what one desires, and to be afflicted by the things in which one exists, and not to be able to escape these things. But to be tormented by punishments is nothing other than to suffer something contrary to oneself against one's will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ob decorem autem universitatis, creavit Deus duo, [Col. 1207A] et duo Eccli. XXXIV, 15. Contra malum bonum est, et contra mortem vita: sic et contra unum justum peccator. Et sic intuere omnia opera Altissimi duo et duo et unum contra unum. ] , sibi contraria, ut aquam igni, et terram aeri; et in his creata alia, de contrariis dolorem patiuntur, sicut in aquis vel in terris formata in igne cruciantur. Salamandra quippe vivit in flamma, moritur in unda; piscis autem in aqua nutritus, in igne moritur.
chunks.english: But for the beauty of the universe, God created two and two things, as Ecclesiasticus chapter thirty-four, verse fifteen says: Against evil is good, and against death is life, so also against the just person is the sinner. And thus contemplate all the works of the Most High, two and two and one against one. He created things contrary to each other, like water against fire and earth against air. And other things created in these suffer pain from contraries, just as things formed in water or earth are tormented in fire. For the salamander lives in flame and dies in water, while the fish nourished in water dies in fire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Rationali itaque naturae, sentire dolorum amaritudinem, est, non habere suavitatis beatitudinem. Ratio namque justitiae poscit ut semper sint in amaritudine miseriae, qui sponte se exuerunt beatitudine gloriae. Ut autem hoc sine fine sit, est haec causa: quod immortales creati sint.
chunks.english: Therefore for rational nature, to feel the bitterness of pains is to not have the sweetness of blessedness. For the principle of justice demands that those who willingly stripped themselves of the blessedness of glory should always be in the bitterness of misery. And that this should be without end, this is the reason: that they were created immortal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cum ab aeterno esset a Deo dispositum, ut Filius ejus incarnaretur, videtur necessario homo peccasse, quatenus propositum Dei compleretur. Si [Col. 1207B] enim ipse non peccasset, Filius Dei incarnatus non esset; sicque statutum Dei irritum fieret. Ergo ex necessitate videtur utrumque pendere; et illum peccasse, et istum incarnatum fuisse.
chunks.english: Student: Since it was arranged by God from eternity that his Son would be incarnated, it seems man necessarily sinned so that God's purpose would be fulfilled. For if man had not sinned, the Son of God would not have been incarnated, and thus God's decree would be made void. Therefore both seem to depend on necessity: that man sinned and that the Son was incarnated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Deus praescivit ab aeterno hominis lapsum, et ideo ab aeterno proposuit Filium suum pro ejus redemptione incarnandum. Porro homo nulla necessitate, sed sola libera voluntate peccavit: et ideo Dominus peccatum illi imputavit: qui si necessario peccasset, reus non esset, cum hoc faceret quod vitare non posset.
chunks.english: Master: God foreknew from eternity man's fall, and therefore from eternity he purposed that his Son would be incarnated for his redemption. But man sinned by no necessity, but by free will alone, and therefore the Lord imputed sin to him. For if he had sinned by necessity, he would not be guilty, since he would be doing what he could not avoid.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quia Dominus eum de peccato praemonuit, et ei poenam mortis proposuit, et ipse divina monita contempsit, idcirco reus exstitit: Quod autem Filius Dei est incarnatus, seu pro [Col. 1207C] homine immolatus, quantum ad ipsum, sola fuit voluntas; quantum ad hominem, summa necessitas. Nisi enim hic incarnaretur, ille nunquam salvaretur. Necesse ergo fuit homini, ut ille vellet incarnari.
chunks.english: But because the Lord forewarned him about sin and set before him the punishment of death, and he despised the divine warnings, for this reason he became guilty. But that the Son of God was incarnated, or immolated for man, as far as he himself was concerned, was will alone; as far as man was concerned, it was absolute necessity. For unless the Son became incarnate, man would never be saved. Therefore it was necessary for man that he will to become incarnate.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur non angelus vel homo pro redimendo homine est missus, sed Dei Filius.
chunks.english: Student: Why was not an angel or a man sent to redeem man, but the Son of God?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Ratio justitiae hoc poposcit. Angelus enim pro homine mitti non debuit, quia nihil homo ad angelicam naturam pertinuit. Homo vero mitti non debuit, quia omnis homo peccator fuit, et peccator peccatorem redimere non potuit. Illum ergo mitti oportuit, qui solus sine peccato, Deo hominem reconciliare potuit Rom. III, 10. Quia non est justus quisquam, [Col. 1208D] etc. Et Rom. V, 12. Sicut per unum hominem peccatum in hunc mundum intravit et per peccatum mors, et ita in omnes homines mors pertransiit in quo omnes peccaverunt. ] .
chunks.english: Master: The principle of justice demanded this. For an angel ought not to have been sent for man, because man pertained nothing to angelic nature. But man ought not to have been sent, because every man was a sinner, and a sinner could not redeem a sinner. Therefore it was necessary that he be sent who alone without sin could reconcile man to God, as Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter three, verse ten says: Because there is no one just. And Romans chapter five, verse twelve: As through one man sin entered into this world and through sin death, and so death passed to all men in whom all sinned.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur nec Pater, nec Spiritus sanctus; sed [Col. 1207D] solus Filius est incarnatus?
chunks.english: Student: Why was neither the Father nor the Holy Spirit, but the Son alone incarnated?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Justitia hoc exegit. Ipse enim quasi secundo loco post Patrem, est Deus, non dignitate, sed ordine. Aequalis est enim Deo Patri; ut puta, unum cum eo. Unde et similitudo Dei dicitur; diabolus autem sibi hoc privilegium usurpavit, dum similis Altissimo esse voluit, scilicet Deus dici, et ab angelis, ut Deus adorari. Et quia in coelo ab angelis hoc non obtinuit, in mundo ab hominibus, se ut Deum coli, et ut Deum adorari docuit. Oportuit ergo, ut [Col. 1208A] cui injuria facta est, veniret; et se verum Deum, et illum fallacem ostenderet.
chunks.english: Master: Justice demanded this. For he is God in a second place after the Father, not in dignity but in order. For he is equal to God the Father, that is, one with him. From this he is also called the likeness of God. But the devil usurped this privilege for himself when he wanted to be like the Most High, namely to be called God and to be adored by angels as God. And because he did not obtain this from angels in heaven, in the world he taught men to worship him as God and to adore him as God. Therefore it was necessary that he to whom the injury was done should come, and show himself to be the true God and the devil to be a deceiver.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et cur in homine venit?
chunks.english: Student: And why did he come as man?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Ut ipse innocens homo pro homine reo (Hebr. I, 33) , Deo Patri satisfaceret et per hominem tyrannum reprimeret, atque hominem ab eo deceptum erueret.
chunks.english: Master: So that he, an innocent man, might make satisfaction to God the Father for guilty man, as the letter to the Hebrews, chapter one, verse thirty-three states, and through man might restrain the tyrant, and rescue man who had been deceived by him.
chunks.notes: The citation appears erroneous in the original; Hebrews has only 14 verses in chapter 1

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Per quid perdidit diabolus hominem quem quondam possedit?
chunks.english: Student: Through what did the devil lose man whom he once possessed?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Per justitiam Dei et potentiam. In potentiam enim Dei, ut caecus graviter offendit; quia jam non solum Deo se aequavit; sed super Deum se exaltavit (Isa. XIV, 14) , dum se a Dei Filio, Patri coaequali in tentatione adorari imperavit. Offensam vero justitiae Dei graviter in passione incurrit, dum [Col. 1208B] justum et innocentem hominem occidit.
chunks.english: Master: Through God's justice and power. For as one blind, he gravely offended the power of God, because now he not only made himself equal to God but exalted himself above God, as Isaiah chapter fourteen, verse fourteen records, when he commanded that he be worshiped by the Son of God, coequal with the Father, in the temptation. But he gravely incurred offense against God's justice in the passion, when he killed a just and innocent man.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ergo per justitiam expertus est potentiam. Nam, dum justus homo injuste occiditur, injustus tyrannus juste opprimitur; et homo deceptus, injuste oppressus, per justitiam redimitur, atque per potentiam super angelum (quem ut Deum coluit) constituitur. Unde adhuc prosperae res secundae dicuntur; quia per justitiam pervenitur ad aeternam potentiam.
chunks.english: Therefore through justice he experienced power. For when a just man is unjustly killed, the unjust tyrant is justly oppressed, and deceived man, unjustly oppressed, is redeemed through justice and established through power above the angel whom he worshiped as God. From this, favorable circumstances are still called secondary, because through justice one arrives at eternal power.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Est in infantibus liberum arbitrium?
chunks.english: Student: Is there free will in infants?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Naturaliter inest eis liberum arbitrium; sed ut ignis in silice latens, nihil in eis operatur.
chunks.english: Master: Free will is naturally present in them, but like fire hidden in flint, it accomplishes nothing in them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur non omnes pueri pro redemptione salvantur, in quibus liberum arbitrium adhuc nihil [Col. 1208C] operatur?
chunks.english: Student: Why are not all children saved through redemption, in whom free will as yet accomplishes nothing?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hoc ex ratione justitiae procedit, quia nihil homini praeter poenam debitum est.
chunks.english: Master: This proceeds from the principle of justice, because nothing is owed to man except punishment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et quare plurimi salvantur, in quibus aeque liberum arbitrium nihil operatur?
chunks.english: Student: And why are very many saved in whom free will equally accomplishes nothing?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hoc autem ex gratia descendit, quae etiam immeritis sua dona tribuit. Si alicujus civitatis populus regem offenderit, ipse quosdam juste puniret, quosdam clementer absolveret; sic cum totus mundus Deum offendisset, de quibusdam supplicium per justitiam sumpsit; quibusdam autem per misericordiam ignovit.
chunks.english: Master: But this descends from grace, which also bestows its gifts on the undeserving. If the people of some city offended the king, he would justly punish some and mercifully absolve others. So when the whole world offended God, he exacted punishment through justice on some, but forgave others through mercy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Unde est quod jam multi liberi arbitrii [Col. 1208D] compotes salvantur, et plurimi non salvantur?
chunks.english: Student: From what source is it that now many who possess free will are saved, and very many are not saved?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Eadem causa est quae dicta est. Illi qui salvantur, ex gratia et libero arbitrio, et ex dono Dei, et non ex proprio merito salvantur homines. Ex gratia Dei quippe est, quod a Deo praeventi bonum volunt; ex libero arbitrio, quod bonum non deserunt. Cujus boni perseverantia, dono Dei, et merito hominis ascribitur: pro quo et vitae praemio remunerabitur. Qui enim perseveraverit usque in finem, salvus erit (Matth. XXIV, 13) .
chunks.english: Master: The reason is the same as was stated. Those who are saved are saved from grace and free will, and from the gift of God, and not from their own merit. For it is from God's grace that, being preceded by God, they want the good; it is from free will that they do not abandon the good. Perseverance in this good is attributed both to God's gift and to man's merit, for which he will be rewarded with the prize of life. For whoever perseveres to the end will be saved, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-four, verse thirteen declares.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: [Col. 1209A] DISC. Unde est quod quidam inveterati in malis tandem salvantur?
chunks.english: Student: From what source is it that some who have grown old in evils are finally saved?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Ex gratia et libero arbitrio: ex gratia quippe praevenienti bona voluntas divinitus gratis inspiratur; ex libero arbitrio ipsi oblatae cooperantur, et pro hoc merito salvantur.
chunks.english: Master: From grace and free will. For from prevenient grace, good will is divinely inspired as a gift; from free will they cooperate with what is offered to them, and for this merit they are saved.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec duo, ut anima et corpus, simul sunt. Per gratiam namque arbitrium (ut corpus per animam) vivificatur; arbitrium vero gratiae, ut corpus animae, cooperatur. Gratia sine libero arbitrio est per se potens ad salvandum; liberum arbitrium sine gratia, est impotens ad bonum operandum.
chunks.english: These two are together like soul and body. For through grace, free will is vivified, as body through soul. But free will cooperates with grace as body with soul. Grace without free will is powerful in itself to save; free will without grace is powerless to work good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Igitur per gratiam liberum arbitrium multum operatur, cujus perseverantia primo coronatur. Gratia est, quod per plures annos, dum a pueritia usque [Col. 1209B] ad decrepitum, in malitia insatiabiliter grassantur, in ipso vitae exitu per poenitentiam ab ipsis diaboli faucibus rapiuntur, et paradisi amoenitate confruendi deducuntur.
chunks.english: Therefore through grace, free will accomplishes much, and its perseverance is crowned first. It is grace that, through many years, while from childhood to decrepit old age they rage insatiably in wickedness, at the very end of life they are snatched through repentance from the very jaws of the devil and led to enjoy the pleasantness of paradise.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Et cur alii non salvantur?
chunks.english: Student: And why are others not saved?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hoc ex justitia et libero arbitrio esse non ignoratur. Ex justitia; quia gratia ad bonum non emolliuntur, sed indurari permittuntur, licet saepius fons gratiae in eos affluat, et eis poenitentiam ad horam tribuat, quam ipsi mox abjiciunt, et ut canes ad vomitum redeunt (Prov. XXVI, 11; Petr. II, 22) .
chunks.english: Master: This is known to come from justice and free will. From justice, because they are not softened by grace unto good, but are permitted to be hardened, although often the fountain of grace flows over them and grants them repentance for a time, which they immediately cast off and return like dogs to their vomit, as the book of Proverbs, chapter twenty-six, verse eleven, and Peter's second letter, chapter two, verse twenty-two record.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex libero arbitrio; quia justitiam oblatam recipere nolunt, et iniquitatem magis deligunt. Illis supradictis est verbum Dei causa salutis, et [Col. 1209C] via ad vitam; istis autem, causa mortis et testimonium ad interitum. Illi sunt vasa misericordiae, praeparata in honorem; isti vasa irae (Rom. IX, 22) , praeparata in contumeliam. De illis nemo perire poterit; de istis nemo salvus erit. Illis est praeparatum regnum a constitutione mundi; istis caminus ignis cum diabolo et angelis ejus Matth. XXV, 34. Monachi et eremitae. ] .
chunks.english: From free will, because they refuse to receive the justice offered to them, and choose iniquity instead. For those mentioned above, the word of God is a cause of salvation and the way to life; but for these others, it is a cause of death and testimony to destruction. The former are vessels of mercy, prepared for honor; the latter are vessels of wrath, as Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter nine, verse twenty-two states, prepared for dishonor. Of the former, no one will be able to perish; of the latter, no one will be saved. For the former, a kingdom is prepared from the foundation of the world; for the latter, a furnace of fire with the devil and his angels, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse thirty-four declares.
chunks.notes: The marginal note 'Monachi et eremitae' appears to be a scribal note indicating the next section topic

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex hac varietate evenit quod plerique in monasteriis sub magna districtione vitam transigunt; quamplurimi in eremo degentes summa abstinentia vitam solitariam ducunt; ad extremum ad ima barathri descendunt.
chunks.english: From this variety it happens that many pass their lives in monasteries under great strictness, and very many dwelling in the desert lead a solitary life with supreme abstinence, yet in the end descend to the depths of the abyss.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Hoc est omni admiratione stupendum.
chunks.english: Student: This is stupefying beyond all wonder.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Accipe causas singulorum. Qui in monasteriis [Col. 1209D] pereunt, in sua prudentia confidunt; praelatorum monita contemnunt; in inobedientia obeunt. Qui vero in eremo intereunt, sine discretione vivunt, quando sibi eligunt ut sanctorum sectentur exempla, patris praecepta despiciendo aspernantur. De his scribitur: Sunt viae quae videntur hominibus justae, quarum finis deducit ad interitum Prov. XIV, 12, et XV, 25. Est via quae videtur homini justa, novissima autem ejus deducunt ad mortem. ] .
chunks.english: Master: Receive the causes of each. Those who perish in monasteries trust in their own prudence, despise the warnings of their superiors, and die in disobedience. But those who perish in the desert live without discretion, when they choose for themselves to follow the examples of the saints while scorning and rejecting their father's precepts. Of these it is written in the book of Proverbs, chapters fourteen, verse twelve, and fifteen, verse twenty-five: There are ways that seem just to men, whose end leads to destruction.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quamobrem in eadem culpa pares, quidam salvantur, quidam reprobantur?
chunks.english: Student: Why are some saved and some reproved when they are equal in the same fault?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Hinc incomprehensibilia Dei judicia praedicantur Rom. XI, 33. O altitudo divitiarum. ] , qui terribilis in consiliis super filios hominum (Psal. LXV, 5) affirmatur: qui eligit per misericordiam, [Col. 1210A] quem vult: et reprobat per justitiam, quem vult, cui nemo potest dicere: cur ita facis? Cujus universae viae misericordia et veritas (Psal. XXIV, 10) .
chunks.english: Master: From this the incomprehensible judgments of God are proclaimed, as Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter eleven, verse thirty-three says: O the depth of riches. He is affirmed to be terrible in his counsels over the sons of men, as Psalm sixty-five, verse five declares. He chooses through mercy whom he wills, and reproves through justice whom he wills, to whom no one can say: Why do you do this? All his ways are mercy and truth, as Psalm twenty-four, verse ten states.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Utrum magis gratiae, an libero arbitrio salus ascribitur?
chunks.english: Student: Is salvation attributed more to grace or to free will?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Summa salutis gratiae Dei attribuitur; cui etiam merita ascribuntur; quia nemo potest venire ad Filium ( qui est vita aeterna ) nisi Pater per gratiam, id est Spiritum sanctum, attraxerit eum (Joan. VI, 44) . Sine illo enim nihil possumus facere (Joan. XV, 5) . Deus quippe in nobis operatur et velle et posse, pro bona voluntate Philip. II, 13. Operatur velle et percefire, etc] .
