chunks.english: Master: The summit of salvation is attributed to God's grace, to which also merits are ascribed, because no one can come to the Son, who is eternal life, unless the Father through grace, that is the Holy Spirit, draws him, as the Gospel of John, chapter six, verse forty-four declares. For without him we can do nothing, as the Gospel of John, chapter fifteen, verse five states. For God works in us both the willing and the doing for good will, as Paul's letter to the Philippians, chapter two, verse thirteen declares.
chunks.notes: The Latin marginal note 'Operatur velle et percefire' appears garbled

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Si Deus operatur, quae merces homini imputatur? [Col. 1210B] MAG. Et Deus operatur, et electi cooperantur. Deus operatur in electis suis, sua gratia praeveniendo velle, et subsequendo posse. Cooperantur ipsi per liberum arbitrium, consentiendo bona voluntate.
chunks.english: Student: If God works, what reward is imputed to man? Master: Both God works and the elect cooperate. God works in his elect, by his grace going before them to will and following to enable. They cooperate through free will by consenting with good will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec bona voluntas remuneratur in eis, ut scriptum est: Accepimus gratiam pro gratia (Joan. I, 16) . Gratiam accipimus, cum nos Deus praevenit, ut velimus; et subsequitur, ut possimus. Pro hac gratia illam gratiam dabit; cum nos in gloria remunerabit.
chunks.english: This good will is rewarded in them, as it is written in the Gospel of John, chapter one, verse sixteen: We have received grace for grace. We receive grace when God goes before us so that we may will, and follows so that we may be able. For this grace he will give that grace when he will reward us in glory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Solent quidam dicere, quod praedestinati necessario salventur. Quod si ita est, cur quidquam laborant? aut cur gentibus praedicatur ut convertantur? vel cur verbum admonitionis quotidie a sacerdotibus administratur populo, ut in proposito [Col. 1210C] Dei proficiant servitio?
chunks.english: Student: Some are accustomed to say that the predestined are necessarily saved. If this is so, why do they labor at all? Or why is it preached to the gentiles that they should convert? Or why is the word of admonition administered daily by priests to the people so that they may advance in service to God's purpose?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Praedestinatio quidem Dei neminem violenter salvat, vel damnat. Olim pro meritis ad regnum praedestinati, tunc quidem necessario salvantur, cum ex justitia proposita gloria eorum meritis recompensatur: sed quia nullus se praedestinatum praesumit (cum nec vas electionis Paulus hoc de se praesumpserit) necesse est toto conamine labori incumbere, quo valeant praedestinationem obtinere.
chunks.english: Master: God's predestination violently saves or damns no one. Those once predestined to the kingdom for their merits are indeed necessarily saved when from justice the proposed glory is recompensed to their merits. But because no one presumes himself predestined—since not even Paul, the vessel of election, presumed this of himself—it is necessary to apply oneself to labor with all effort, by which they may be able to obtain predestination.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per laborem namque praedestinatio vitae adipiscitur, ut in sacra auctoritate dicitur - Per multas tribulationes oportet nos introire in regnum Dei (Act. XIII, 21) . Infantibus itaque per mortis acerbitatem; juvenibus [Col. 1210D] per operis exercitationem in extremis poenitentiam agentibus, datur praedestinatio per purgatorii cruciatus examinationem.
chunks.english: For through labor, predestination of life is obtained, as it is said in sacred authority: Through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God, as the Acts of the Apostles, chapter thirteen, verse twenty-one declares. Therefore to infants through the bitterness of death, to the young through the exercise of work, to those doing penance in their final moments, predestination is given through examination by the torments of purgatory.
chunks.notes: The citation appears to be Acts 14:22, not 13:21 in standard Bible editions

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Gentibus vero ideo praedicatur, quia praedestinati foris, in foro otiose stantes in vineam Domini ad laborem convocantur (Matth. X, 6) . Quibus etiam denarius in mercede proponitur: quia praedestinata gloria usque in finem laborantibus dabitur.
chunks.english: But to the gentiles it is preached because the predestined who are outside, standing idle in the marketplace, are called into the Lord's vineyard for labor, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter ten, verse six indicates. To whom also a denarius is proposed as reward, because the predestined glory will be given to those laboring to the end.
chunks.notes: The parable reference is actually Matthew 20, not chapter 10

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe quoque quidam de reprobis, cum eis in vineam ingrediuntur; sed in initio laboris ab opere deficiunt, et aut de vinca blasphemantes exeunt, aut in vinea manentes, laborantes impediunt. De [Col. 1211A] his dicitur: Ex nobis exierunt: sed non erant ex nobis I Joan. II, 19. Ex nobis prodierunt. ] . Et iterum: Multiplicati sunt super numerum (Psal. XXXIX, 6) : scilicet ad vitam praedestinatorum.
chunks.english: Often also some of the reprobate enter the vineyard with them, but in the beginning of labor they fail from the work, and either leave the vineyard blaspheming, or remaining in the vineyard, they impede those laboring. Of these it is said in John's first letter, chapter two, verse nineteen: They went out from us, but they were not of us. And again in Psalm thirty-nine, verse six: They have multiplied beyond number—namely beyond the number of those predestined to life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Multi quidem per fidem sagenae Petri inhaeserunt (Matth. XIII, 47) ; sed quia in numero electorum praecogniti non sunt, in fluctus vitiorum, rupto reti, resilierunt.
chunks.english: Many indeed clung through faith to Peter's net, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter thirteen, verse forty-seven describes, but because they were not foreknown in the number of the elect, they sprang back into the waves of vices when the net broke.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Populus autem fidelium, in labore pii operis fatigatus, verbo admonitionis, ne deficiat, sublevatur. Sicut aeger ad ecclesiam vadens, sed in via deficiens, ut iter peragat, ab aliquo sustentatur.
chunks.english: But the people of the faithful, wearied in the labor of pious work, are lifted up by the word of admonition lest they fail, just as a sick person going to church, but failing on the way, is sustained by someone so that he may complete the journey.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verbum quippe Dei est verum semen (Luc. VIII, 11) ; ager vero, corda hominum. Cum hoc semen per Dei agricultores spargitur; semen Dei gratia est. [Col. 1211B] Si autem ab agricultura recipitur, liberum arbitrium est: quod per fidem receptum, si germen bonorum operum emiserit, messis in praedestinata gloria erit.
chunks.english: For the word of God is the true seed, as the Gospel of Luke, chapter eight, verse eleven declares; but the field is the hearts of men. When this seed is scattered by God's farmers, the seed is God's grace. But if it is received by the agriculture, it is free will. And this, received through faith, if it sends forth the sprout of good works, the harvest will be in predestined glory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cum haec cuncta, perspicua ratio, de luce veritatis proferat, oportet ut cunctis studiosis, per omnia valde complaceant. Porro me multum movet, quod angelica natura cadere potuit, si ad beatitudinem condita fuit: videtur enim, quod quaedam vis eam impulerit, quod ita irrecuperabiliter corruit. Nam nimis de lapsu hominis moveor quod de tanta gloria in tantam miseriam devenire potuit, si nulla necessitas eum impulit. [Col. 1211C] Unde rogo ut effundas cor tuum, et eructes veritatis arcanum.
chunks.english: Student: Since clear reason brings forth all these things from the light of truth, they ought to please all the studious greatly in all respects. But I am much moved that angelic nature could fall if it was created for blessedness, for it seems that some force impelled it, that it fell so irrecoverably. For I am exceedingly moved about the fall of man, that he could descend from such great glory into such great misery if no necessity impelled him. Therefore I beg that you pour out your heart and bring forth the secret of truth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Breviter tibi pandam Dei occulta, et reclusa cordis diligenter ausculta.
chunks.english: Master: I will briefly lay open to you the hidden things of God, and listen carefully with the opened heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antequam Deus conderet mundum, praescivit et angeli et hominis casum: quem ideo fieri permisit, quia bonum malo illustrari censuit.
chunks.english: Before God created the world, he foreknew both the fall of angel and of man. He permitted this to happen because he judged that good should be illuminated by evil.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praescivit etiam, qui et quot secum essent permansuri, qui et quot essent recessuri, qui et quot ad se reversuri. Si enim hoc ignorasset, praescius futurorum non esset. Et si certus numerus electorum non esset, tunc regnum Dei, non ordinata dispositione, sed fortuito casu constaret, ad quod incerti numeri frequentia conflueret. Sed cum apud Deum sit certus numerus capillorum Matth. X, 30 et Luc. XXII, 7. Capilli capitis vestri omnes numerati sunt. ] , multo [Col. 1211D] magis est apud Deum praefixus numerus electorum.
chunks.english: He also foreknew who and how many would remain with him, who and how many would depart, who and how many would return to him. For if he had not known this, he would not be foreknowing of future things. And if there were not a fixed number of the elect, then the kingdom of God would consist not by ordered disposition but by random chance, to which a crowd of uncertain number would flow together. But since with God there is a fixed number of hairs, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter ten, verse thirty, and Luke, chapter twenty-two, verse seven declare: All the hairs of your head are numbered, much more is there with God a predetermined number of the elect.
chunks.notes: The Luke citation appears erroneous; this saying is in Luke 12:7, not 22:7

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quot autem sunt in hoc numero a Deo praescripti hi ante mundi constitutionem sunt ad beatitudinem electi. De his nullus peribit, sed ad praedestinatam gloriam toto conamine quisque festinabit. Qui autem super hunc numerum multiplicantur, inter oves Christi non numerantur.
chunks.english: But however many are inscribed in this number by God, these were chosen for blessedness before the foundation of the world. Of these, no one will perish, but each one will hasten with all effort to predestined glory. But those who are multiplied beyond this number are not numbered among the sheep of Christ.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia hic numerus angelis cadentibus est imminutus, hominibus nascentibus est restitutus. Et ideo sicut ab uno numerare incipimus, sic ab uno [Col. 1212A] homine numerus est incoeptus. Et, sicut numerus crescit ad perfectionem, ita propago humana successit usque ad electorum completionem.
chunks.english: And because this number was diminished by the angels falling, it was restored by humans being born. And therefore just as we begin to count from one, so from one man the number was begun. And as the number grows to perfection, so human offspring succeeded until the completion of the elect.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hic ergo sacer numerus, soli Deo cognitus, et ab eo, aeterna certitudine praefixus, est quasi cujusdam civitatis ambitus, intra quem necesse sit omnes contineri, cui hujus civitatis cives ab aeterno sunt praecogniti. Lex autem civitatis, inviolabilis justitia est a justissimo rege instituta, ac hujus servatores, justi cognominantur, et hi soli inter cives connumerantur.
chunks.english: Therefore this sacred number, known to God alone and predetermined by him with eternal certainty, is like the boundary of a certain city, within which all must be contained, whose citizens of this city have been foreknown from eternity. But the law of the city is inviolable justice instituted by the most just king, and the keepers of this are called just, and these alone are numbered among the citizens.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Privilegium hujus sacrae civitatis, est libertas. Regnum ejus, beatitudo. Ergo omnes habitatores hujus civitatis sunt justi, liberi erant, legem hujus civitatis servare nolebant. Et ideo inter cives ejus numerari non poterant. Ad Dominium autem [Col. 1212B] ejus aspirabant, dum uterque ut Deus in ea esse appetebat.
chunks.english: The privilege of this sacred city is freedom. Its kingdom is blessedness. Therefore all the inhabitants of this city are just and were free, but they did not want to keep the law of this city. And therefore they could not be numbered among its citizens. But they aspired to dominion over it, while each desired to be as God in it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Igitur nulla vis; nulla necessitas, eos ab hac urbe expulit; sed lex justitiae violatores juris in ea cives esse vetuit.
chunks.english: Therefore no force, no necessity expelled them from this city, but the law of justice forbade violators of the law to be citizens in it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. O quam in excelsam speculam me duxisti, in qua plurima conspicio! sed quia adhuc aliqua sunt clausa, illa mihi aperiri postulo.
chunks.english: Student: O how high a watchtower you have led me to, in which I see very many things! But because some things are still closed, I demand that they be opened to me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si homo per liberum arbitrium cadit, cur per liberum arbitrium non resurgit?
chunks.english: If man falls through free will, why does he not rise again through free will?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Homo potest se de alto monte in profundum praecipitium per seipsum mittere; non potest autem, nisi per alium adjutus, redire. Ita per liberum arbitrium, justitiam quidam deserere; non per se autem, sed per solam gratiam potest eam recipere. A [Col. 1212C] quo enim quis vincitur, illius et servus efficitur II Petr. II, 19. A quo quis superatus est, [Col. 1212D] hujus et servus est. ] .
chunks.english: Master: Man can throw himself from a high mountain into a deep precipice by himself, but he cannot return unless helped by another. So through free will, someone can abandon justice, but not by himself, but only through grace alone can he receive it back. For by whom someone is conquered, of that one he also becomes a servant, as Peter's second letter, chapter two, verse nineteen states: By whom someone is overcome, of that one he is also a servant.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam qui sponte luxuriae vel cuilibet vitio, cui praeest, addicitur; jam non valet per liberum arbitrium se de jugo dominationis ejus excutere, cujus dominio se libere voluit subjicere, nisi gratia Dei praeveniat eum, ut bonum quod sprevit cupiat; et subsequatur, ut illud implere praevaleat.
chunks.english: For he who willingly addicts himself to luxury or to any vice over which it presides can no longer through free will shake off the yoke of its domination, to whose dominion he freely chose to subject himself, unless God's grace goes before him so that he desires the good which he scorned, and follows so that he may prevail to fulfill it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cum voluntas hominis sit facta libera, unde est ad malum tam prona, et ad bonum tam pigra?
chunks.english: Student: Since man's will was made free, why is it so prone to evil and so sluggish toward good?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Omnis rationalis voluntas naturaliter cupit summum bonum; sed errore decepta labitur in falsum, quod est malum. Quilibet enim vult esse beatus, et ideo id quisque quam maxime appetit, in [Col. 1212D] quo se sperat fore beatum. Sed quia summum bonum, vel beatitudo, nonnisi in solo Deo consistit; quisquis aliquid infra Deum positum pro summo bono appetit, errat.
chunks.english: Master: Every rational will naturally desires the supreme good, but deceived by error it slips into the false, which is evil. For everyone wants to be happy, and therefore each person most greatly desires that in which he hopes to be happy. But because the supreme good, or blessedness, consists in God alone, whoever desires something placed below God as the supreme good errs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hujus autem erroris causa haec est: Deus condidit hominem justum et beatum, sine omni indigentia, in bonorum omnium sufficientia Eccl. VII, 30. Inveni quod Deus fecerit hominem rectum, et ipse infinitis miscuerit quaestionibus. ] , et dedit liberam voluntatem, justitiae et beatitudinis, ut voluntate justitiae, corpori subdito imperaret; voluntate beatitudinis, Deo obediret. Habuit ergo justitiam, ad honorem Dei; beatitudinem, ad commodum [Col. 1213A] suum. Et si servata justitia Deum honorasset, ad summam angelorum beatitudinem pervenisset.
chunks.english: But the cause of this error is this: God created man just and blessed, without any need, in the sufficiency of all good things, as Ecclesiastes chapter seven, verse thirty declares: I found that God made man upright, and he himself mixed infinite questions. And God gave free will of justice and blessedness, so that by the will of justice he would rule over the subjected body, and by the will of blessedness he would obey God. Therefore he had justice for God's honor, and blessedness for his own advantage. And if, having kept justice, he had honored God, he would have attained to the supreme blessedness of the angels.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quia justitiam deseruit, beatitudinem amisit. Sed voluntatem beatitudinis retinuit. Quia ergo fervet desiderio commodorum; sed commoda rationalis naturae competentia, quae perdidit, habere non valet, ad falsa et brutorum commoda et bestiales appetitus se convertit. Et quia jumentis insipientibus similis factus est Psal. XLVIII, 13. Homo cum in honore esset, non intellexit; comparatus est jumentis insipientibus et similis factus est illis. ] : ideo per concupiscentiam ut bestia coit; cum dolore parit; lacte infantes, ut bestia catulos, nutrit; et sola commoda corporis appetit.
chunks.english: But because he abandoned justice, he lost blessedness. Yet he retained the will for blessedness. Therefore, because he burns with desire for advantages, but cannot have the advantages befitting rational nature which he lost, he turns to false and brutish advantages and bestial appetites. And because he was made like senseless beasts, as Psalm forty-eight, verse thirteen declares: Man when he was in honor did not understand; he was compared to senseless beasts and made like them—therefore through concupiscence he mates like a beast, gives birth with pain, nurses infants with milk like a beast nurses cubs, and desires only the advantages of the body.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur bestiae pro his non damnantur?
chunks.english: Student: Why are beasts not damned for these things?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Appetitus in brutis animalibus non sunt peccatum; sed sunt naturales, in hominibus vero [Col. 1213B] irrationales: caetera namque animantia feruntur necessitate; homo regitur libertate. Unde scribitur: Qui hoc fecerit, quod homo facere non debet, tollatur de hominibus.
chunks.english: Master: Appetites in brute animals are not sin but are natural, but in humans they are irrational. For other living creatures are driven by necessity; man is governed by freedom. From this it is written: Whoever does this, which man ought not to do, let him be removed from among men.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid est, quod quidam immenso conatu nituntur, ut bene vivant; et nihil proficiunt? aut post magnum profectum a proposito decidunt? Nonne aliqua occulta vis eos retrahit, ne coeptum opus implere possint?
chunks.english: Student: What is it that some strive with immense effort to live well and accomplish nothing? Or after great progress they fall from their purpose? Does not some hidden force draw them back so that they cannot complete the work they began?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Nulla eos necessitas retrahit; sed sola voluntas eos avertit, dum magis volunt quod suggerit eis tentatio, quam quod persuadet ratio. Quidam etiam de arbitrio magis quam de gratia praesumunt; et cuncta suis meritis ascribunt: [Col. 1213C] ideo eos gratia deserente, juste laborem suum perdunt.
chunks.english: Master: No necessity draws them back, but will alone turns them away, when they want more what temptation suggests to them than what reason persuades. Some also presume more on their own will than on grace, and attribute all things to their own merits. Therefore, with grace deserting them, they justly lose their labor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur Deus facit hominis arbitrium ita mutabile?
chunks.english: Student: Why does God make man's will so changeable?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Ob magnum ejus proficuum: quod enim potest converti ad malum, potest iterato converti ad bonum. Si homo semel lapsus non esset mutabilis, nunquam esset ad bonum convertibilis.
chunks.english: Master: For his great advantage. For what can be turned to evil can again be turned to good. If man once fallen were not changeable, he would never be convertible to good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quare non fecit eum Deus, ut in bono esset immutabilis?
chunks.english: Student: Why did God not make him so that he would be unchangeable in good?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Tunc esset Deo aequalis; hoc enim solius Dei est.
chunks.english: Master: Then he would be equal to God; for this belongs to God alone.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Si quisque operatur libero arbitrio, quomodo operatur Deus omnia in omnibus?
chunks.english: Student: If each person works by free will, how does God work all things in all?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Universa quae fiunt bona, sive in coelo, sive [Col. 1213D] in terra, seu in omni creatura, Deus solus bonus, per electos vel angelos vel homines operatur, cooperantibus eis per liberum arbitrium consentiendo.
chunks.english: Master: All good things that happen, whether in heaven or on earth or in every creature, God alone who is good works through the elect, whether angels or men, with them cooperating by consenting through free will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Operatur Deus per electos, ut per apostolos gentes convertit; ipse interius inspirando et incrementum dando: cooperantur ipsi per exterius ministerium, plantando et rigando.
chunks.english: God works through the elect, as he converted the gentiles through the apostles, he himself inspiring within and giving increase. They cooperate through external ministry, planting and watering.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si quid autem electi, contrarii egerunt, ut in [Col. 1214A] Uria David (II Reg. XI, 1) , Deus hoc juste fieri permittit, quod tamen ipse in laudem sui convertit, dum post lapsum exstiterunt humiliores, et sibi grates referunt uberiores; quibus etiam ipsa peccata cooperantur in bonum Rom. VIII, 28. Diligentibus Deum omnia cooperantur in bonum. ] . Ergo omnia ex ipso et per ipsum (Rom. XI, 36) .
chunks.english: But if the elect have done anything contrary, as David did with Uriah in the second book of Kings, chapter eleven, verse one, God justly permits this to happen, which nevertheless he himself converts to his praise, since after the fall they became more humble and render more abundant thanks to him. For them even sins themselves cooperate unto good, as Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter eight, verse twenty-eight states: For those who love God, all things cooperate unto good. Therefore all things are from him and through him, as Romans chapter eleven, verse thirty-six declares.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per reprobos quoque Deus operatur, dum judicium suum per eos exercet, sicut per Chaldaeos Hierusalem destruxit (Jer. LII, 24) . Ecce unum et idem opus Deus et Chaldaei, diverso modo operati sunt; et tamen hic inde laudatur, et illi damnantur. Et quod ipse per justitiam, hoc ipsi per saevitiam egerunt.
chunks.english: Through the reprobate also God works when he exercises his judgment through them, just as through the Chaldeans he destroyed Jerusalem, as Jeremiah chapter fifty-two, verse twenty-four records. Behold, one and the same work God and the Chaldeans accomplished in different ways, and yet he is praised for it and they are condemned. And what he did through justice, they did through cruelty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Similiter per Judam Deus Filium suum tradidit (Matth. XXVI, 48) , et mundum a morte redemit. En [Col. 1214B] Deus et Judas, unum opus diversa mente operati sunt. Sed dum totus orbis inde Deum veneratur, Judam vero detestatur; quia quod Deus ob humani generis amicitiam, hoc Judas egit per avaritiam.
chunks.english: Similarly through Judas, God handed over his Son, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-six, verse forty-eight records, and redeemed the world from death. Behold, God and Judas accomplished one work with different minds. But while the whole world venerates God for this and detests Judas, it is because what God did for love of the human race, Judas did through greed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Audenter dico, quia Deus etiam per diabolum operatur, quod de justitia, per eum in reprobis exerceri decernitur. Sed quod Deus per justissimam aequitatem, hoc agit diabolus per nequissimam crudelitatem. Qui tamen non plus potest saevire in eos, quam permittitur. Unde Deus laudabiliter magnificatur; ille vero pro eodem damnabiliter reprobatur.
chunks.english: I boldly say that God even works through the devil, in that what pertains to justice is determined to be exercised through him in the reprobate. But what God does through most just equity, the devil does through most wicked cruelty. Yet he cannot rage against them more than is permitted. From this God is praiseworthily magnified, but the devil is damnably reproved for the same thing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quaecunque autem a reprobis per liberum arbitrium contra instituta committuntur, a Deo quidem [Col. 1214C] fieri sinuntur, et in laudem ejus vertuntur, dum juste ab eo poenis subiguntur. Igitur Deus omnia operatur, aut favendo aut sinendo.
chunks.english: But whatever is committed by the reprobate through free will against what is established is indeed permitted by God to happen and is turned to his praise, when they are justly subjected by him to punishments. Therefore God works all things, either by favoring or by permitting.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia ex ipso, ut in lib. Sap. legitur: A Deo bona et mala Eccli. XI, 14. Bona et mala, vita et mors, paupertas et honestas a Deo sunt. ] , omnia sunt bona, et nihil est malum, nisi quod amarum est his, qui aliquid asperi patiuntur, id malum appellatur. Et ideo omnia ad laudem et gloriam Dei, qui miseretur cui vult Rom. IX, 18. Cujus vult miseretur, et quem vult indurat. ] , gratiam largiendo; et indurat quem vult, in malitia relinquendo.
chunks.english: And because from him, as is read in the book of Wisdom—from God come both good and evil, as Ecclesiasticus chapter eleven, verse fourteen says: Good and evil, life and death, poverty and honor are from God—all things are good, and nothing is evil except what is bitter to those who suffer something harsh, and that is called evil. And therefore all things are to the praise and glory of God, who has mercy on whom he wills, as Paul's letter to the Romans, chapter nine, verse eighteen declares: On whom he wills he has mercy, and whom he wills he hardens, bestowing grace and hardening whom he wills by leaving them in malice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Nunquam haec audita sunt in mundo?
chunks.english: Student: Have these things never been heard in the world?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Imo, quotidie in Scripturis audiuntur Matth. XVIII, 18. Quaecunque solveritis super terram, erunt soluta et in coelo. Et n. 19: Quia si duo ex vobis consenserint super terram, de omni re quamcunque petierint, fiet illis a Patre meo qui in coelis est. Et XXVIII, 19: Euntes docete omnes gentes. ] ; sed a desidiosis et negligentibus non attenduntur: et ideo non intelliguntur. Cum vero in disputatione a doctis haec audierint, quasi a somno excitati stupescunt. [Col. 1214D] Vera esse dubitant, quia nusquam esse scripta putant. A studiosis autem leguntur, audiuntur, attenduntur, discutiuntur et intelliguntur, et in memoriam reducuntur.
chunks.english: Master: Indeed, they are heard daily in the Scriptures, as the Gospel of Matthew, chapter eighteen, verse eighteen says: Whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven. And verse nineteen: Because if two of you consent on earth about any matter whatever they ask, it will be done for them by my Father who is in heaven. And chapter twenty-eight, verse nineteen: Go and teach all nations. But they are not attended to by the lazy and negligent, and therefore are not understood. But when they hear these things from the learned in disputation, they are astonished as if awakened from sleep. They doubt they are true because they think they are written nowhere. But by the studious they are read, heard, attended to, examined, and understood, and brought back to memory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sponsus namque Christus abiens, sponsae Ecclesiae claves scientiae reliquit, quibus quotidie pulsantibus filiis, secreta Dei per intellectum reserans, aperit. A canibus autem, et a porcis, margaritas, ne coinquinentur, claudit.
chunks.english: For the Bridegroom Christ, departing, left to his bride the Church the keys of knowledge, by which daily, to the sons who knock, he opens the secrets of God by unlocking understanding. But from dogs and from swine he closes the pearls lest they be defiled.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: [Col. 1215A] DISC. Cuncta quae proponis, tam validis testimoniis probando per illationem concludis, ut plus caeco probetur errare, qui haec praesumpserit pertinaciter impugnare.
chunks.english: Student: All things which you propose, proving with such strong testimonies and concluding by inference, demonstrate that he errs more than a blind man who has presumed to attack these things obstinately.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed, quaeso, edisseras: Utrum prospera et adversa per liberum arbitrium eveniant.
chunks.english: But I ask, explain: Do prosperity and adversity come about through free will?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Imo per gratiam et justitiam Dei. Gratia quidem Dei, prospera ministrat; justitia autem, adversa dispensat. Sed justi per prospera ad perennia bona provocantur: injusti autem per prospera, ut dives ille Luc., XVI, 25: Fili, recepisti bona in vita tua et Lazarus similiter mala; nunc autem hic consolatur, tu vero cruciaris. ] , remunerantur. Adversa autem ob tres causas electos tangunt. Primo, ut quidam a peccatis per adversa corrigantur, ut David; secundo, ut quidam tentati magis coronentur, ut Job; tertio, ut quidam a delectatione peccati retrahantur, [Col. 1215B] ut Paulus.
chunks.english: Master: Rather through God's grace and justice. God's grace indeed dispenses prosperity, but justice dispenses adversity. But the just are provoked through prosperity to eternal goods, while the unjust are rewarded through prosperity, like that rich man in the Gospel of Luke, chapter sixteen, verse twenty-five: Son, you received good things in your life and Lazarus likewise evil things; but now he is consoled here, but you are tormented. But adversity touches the elect for three reasons. First, so that some may be corrected from sins through adversity, like David; second, so that some who are tested may be crowned more, like Job; third, so that some may be drawn back from the delight of sin, like Paul.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Reprobos vero ob duas causas feriunt. Primo, ut electi per eorum plagas corrigantur, ut in exitio Core legitur; secundo, ut ipsi a malitia, ne tantum quantum volunt, noceant, reprimantur, ut de Antiocho et Herode scribitur.
chunks.english: But they strike the reprobate for two reasons. First, so that the elect may be corrected through their plagues, as is read in the destruction of Korah; second, so that they themselves may be restrained from malice lest they harm as much as they wish, as is written about Antiochus and Herod.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Parietem fodisti, et ecce apparet ostium. Cum Scriptura dicat, quod Pharao a Deo sit induratus Exod. IX, 16. Idcirco posui te ut ostendam in te fortitudinem meam et narretur nomen meum in universa terra. Et Rom. IX, 17: Quia in hoc ipsum excitavi te ut ostendam in te virtutem meam: et ut annuntietur nomen meum in universa terra. ] ; imo in hoc ipsum, ut annuntiaretur nomen Dei in universa terra per eum sit constitutus: quis non videat quod, necessitate cogente, populum afflixerit, cum in hoc ipsum constitutus sit, et quasi quodam fato trahente submersus sit?
chunks.english: Student: You have dug through the wall, and behold a door appears. Since Scripture says that Pharaoh was hardened by God, as the book of Exodus, chapter nine, verse sixteen declares: For this purpose I have raised you up, that I may show my strength in you and that my name may be declared in all the earth. And Romans chapter nine, verse seventeen: Because for this very thing I raised you up, that I might show in you my power, and that my name might be declared in all the earth—indeed, he was established for this very purpose, that God's name might be declared in all the earth through him. Who does not see that, driven by necessity, he afflicted the people, since he was established for this very purpose and was submerged as if drawn by some fate?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Nota tria: Populum afflictum; Pharaonem [Col. 1215C] affligentem; Deum liberantem.
chunks.english: Master: Note three things: The people afflicted, Pharaoh afflicting, God liberating.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deus electos, ut vasa aurea per reprobos, in camino tribulationis, tentando examinat; sed ipsi reprobi non intendunt, quod eorum vexatio istis ad salutem proficiat, quos sola crudelitatis malitia tribulat.
chunks.english: God tests the elect, like golden vessels through the reprobate, in the furnace of tribulation by trying them. But the reprobate themselves do not intend that their vexation might profit those for salvation whom the malice of cruelty alone afflicts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Porro diabolum, qui libere justitiam deseruit, in injustitia, ut incudem malleatorum, indurari permisit, eumque fabrum, ad purganda vasa misericordiae constituit: qui faber omnes impios sibi instrumenta fecit. ex quibus Pharao unus exstitit; qui dum per liberum arbitrium Deo servire noluit, Deus, eum induravit; dum eum a malitia, et duritia non liberavit.
chunks.english: Furthermore, the devil, who freely abandoned justice, God permitted to be hardened in injustice like the anvil of hammers, and established him as a smith for purifying the vessels of mercy. This smith made all the impious his instruments, of whom Pharaoh was one. When through free will he refused to serve God, God hardened him by not freeing him from malice and hardness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: [Col. 1215D] Et cum populus Dei per afflictionem esset tentandus, et adversitate probandus: Deus in hoc ipsum, Pharaonem ad tentandum populum constituit, dum ipse se per liberum arbitrium (ut puta unus de malleis diaboli) ad tundenda vasa Dei obtulit; sicque Pharao nescius, justis, quasi servus [Col. 1216A] filiis, servivit, dum eos a vasis irae segregans, flagellis erudivit.
chunks.english: And when God's people were to be tested through affliction and proved through adversity, God for this very purpose established Pharaoh to test the people, when he offered himself through free will—as one of the devil's hammers, so to speak—to pound the vessels of God. And so Pharaoh, unknowing, served the just like a servant serves sons, while by separating them from the vessels of wrath, he instructed them with scourges.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Diabolus et impii malum quidem per se volunt non autem quantum volunt, sed quantum permittuntur facere poterunt. Et cum a Deo electis praevalere permittuntur, in hoc ipsum constitui dicuntur Videri merito debet in hac materia et modo loquendi D. Greg Exposi. moral. lib. XIII in c. 17 [Col. 1216D] Job, c. 12. Et lib. XXXIV, in c. 41, c. 10.] .
chunks.english: The devil and the impious indeed want evil in themselves, but not as much as they want; rather, only as much as they are permitted can they do. And when they are permitted to prevail over God's elect, they are said to be established for this very purpose. On this matter and manner of speaking, Saint Gregory's Moral Exposition, book thirteen on Job chapter seventeen, chapter twelve, and book thirty-four on Job chapter forty-one, chapter ten, ought rightly to be consulted.
chunks.notes: This appears to be a marginal scholarly note incorporated into the text

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nomen autem Dei per Pharaonem omnibus innotuit, dum ipse cum omnibus suis juste interiit; et Deus ab eo oppressos, ut vasa jam igne examinata in fornace, signis et prodigiis eripuit.
chunks.english: But God's name became known to all through Pharaoh, when he justly perished with all his own, and God rescued those oppressed by him, like vessels already tested by fire in the furnace, with signs and prodigies.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. De Juda quoque scribitur: Ut Scriptura impleatur, Qui manducat panem meum, levavit contra me calcaneum suum Joan. XVI, 18: Qui manducat panem meum, etc.] . Et iterum: Nemo periit ex his quos dedisti mihi, nisi filius perditionis, [Col. 1216B] ut Scriptura impleatur Joan. XVII, 12: Quos dedisti mihi custodivi, et nemo ex eis periit nisi filius perditionis, ut Scriptura impleatur. ] .
chunks.english: Student: Also about Judas it is written: That Scripture might be fulfilled, He who eats my bread has lifted his heel against me, as the Gospel of John, chapter sixteen, verse eighteen records. And again: No one perished of those whom you gave me, except the son of perdition, that Scripture might be fulfilled, as the Gospel of John, chapter seventeen, verse twelve states: Those whom you gave me I have guarded, and no one of them perished except the son of perdition, that Scripture might be fulfilled.
chunks.notes: The first citation is actually John 13:18, not 16:18

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et cum Scripturam, ipso Domino teste, impleri necesse sit, quis nisi per omnia caecus non videat quod Judas quadam vi necessitatis impulsus, Dominum prodiderit; maxime cum Dominus venerat pati, et necesse fuerit eum per aliquem tradi?
chunks.english: And since Scripture, with the Lord himself as witness, must be fulfilled, who unless blind in all things does not see that Judas, driven by some force of necessity, betrayed the Lord, especially since the Lord had come to suffer and it was necessary that he be handed over by someone?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Dominus quantum ad ipsum, non necessitate, sed liberrima voluntate pati voluit, quia natura divina impassibilis fuit: et hoc nobis fieri valde necessarium fuit; quos necessitas mortis ad interitum impulit.
chunks.english: Master: The Lord, as far as he himself was concerned, willed to suffer not by necessity but by most free will, because the divine nature was impassible. And this was very necessary to be done for us, whom the necessity of death drove to destruction.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Judam nulla necessitas, sed malitiosa voluntas instigavit ad proditionem; dum Dominum cum Judaeis odisse, quam cum apostolis diligere maluit. Similiter Judaei nulla necessitate compulsi, sed [Col. 1216C] pessima voluntate impulsi eum occiderunt; dum eum gratis odio habuerunt, quem signis et virtutibus constare doluerunt.
chunks.english: No necessity but malicious will instigated Judas to betrayal, when he preferred to hate the Lord with the Jews rather than to love him with the apostles. Similarly the Jews, compelled by no necessity but driven by the worst will, killed him, when they had gratuitous hatred for him whose signs and virtues they regretted were manifest.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quia Spiritus sanctus, cui omnia futura sunt praesentia, qui talia eos velle facturos praescivit; ita per prophetas in Scripturis praedixit, sicut postmodum totum contigit Psal. CVIII, 8: Fiant dies ejus pauci, et episcopatum ejus accipiat alter. ] : tamen illa Scriptura nullam necessitatem volendi vel faciendi eis intulit. Sicut ego, si bellum praescirem, et illud futurum praedicerem, verba mea non facerent bellum necessarium.
chunks.english: But because the Holy Spirit, to whom all future things are present, foreknew that they would want to do such things, he thus predicted through the prophets in the Scriptures, just as afterward everything happened, as Psalm one hundred eight, verse eight says: Let his days be few, and let another take his office. Nevertheless that Scripture imposed no necessity of willing or doing on them. Just as if I foreknew a war and predicted it would happen, my words would not make the war necessary.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quid igitur quis hinc colligat, nisi necessarium fuisse, Christum a Judaeis crucifigi, qui venerat ab eis occidi, quod utique non fecisset, si facere [Col. 1216D] non debuisset?
chunks.english: Student: What therefore should one gather from this, except that it was necessary for Christ to be crucified by the Jews, who had come to be killed by them, which surely he would not have done if he ought not to have done it?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Et credis tu solos Judaeos Christum occidisse? omnes iniqui ab initio usque in finem mundi, consenserunt in necem Christi Psal. LXXXVI, 5: Multiplicati sunt super capillos
chunks.english: Master: And do you believe that the Jews alone killed Christ? All the wicked from the beginning to the end of the world consented to Christ's death, as Psalm eighty-six, verse five begins: They have multiplied beyond the hairs
chunks.notes: Text appears to be cut off mid-sentence at column break

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: capitis mei qui oderunt me gratis. ] : quot enim justitiam et veritatem odio habent, hi justos propter justitiam et veritatem, qui est, persequuntur, et omnes mortis Domini rei inveniuntur.
chunks.english: of my head who have hated me without cause. All who hate justice and truth thereby persecute the just because of justice and truth, which is Christ himself. And so they are found guilty of the Lord's death.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vellent quippe, si possent, justos omnes delere, quo securi possent sua desideria absque contradictione explere. Ergo si illi non fecissent, similes illorum perfecissent.
chunks.english: For they would gladly destroy all the just, if they could, so that they might pursue their own desires without opposition. Therefore, even if the Jews had not done this deed, others like them would have accomplished it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Introductus per te interius, multa praeclara considero; sed multa sigillata me non posse cernere doleo. Unde rogo ut, tradita tibi clave scientiae, haec clausa mihi aperias, et ista, involuta evolvens, me inspicere facias.
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Led inward by you, I behold many splendid things. But many remain sealed, and I grieve that I cannot perceive them. Therefore I beg you, with the key of knowledge given to you, open what is closed to me. Unfold what is wrapped up, and let me see it clearly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Tam diligenter pulsanti, Dominus per me prospera respondens, arcana secretorum tibi aperiet.
chunks.english: MASTER: To one who knocks so diligently, the Lord will answer favorably through me, and reveal to you the hidden mysteries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quidnam causae esse dicimus, quod Deus permittit eos diu errare, quos praedestinavit, perenniter secum regnare?
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Why do we say that God permits those whom he has predestined to reign with him forever to wander in error for so long?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Per hoc longanimitati patientiae suae praerogat, quod etiam diutius in errorem, ut Paulum, tolerat : quod vero eos repente ad poenitentiam, ut idem Paulum et Mariam revocat ; divitias misericordiae suae insinuat. Pro his in mundum venit; pro his et mortem subiit.
chunks.english: MASTER: By tolerating some in error for a long time, like Paul, God displays the greatness of his patient endurance. And by suddenly calling them to repentance, as he did with Paul and Mary Magdalene, he reveals the riches of his mercy. He came into the world for such people. For such people he even underwent death.
chunks.notes: References Romans 2:4 and Luke 8 (Mary Magdalene's conversion)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quamvis pro peccatoribus mortuus sit, Annae et Caiphae, Herodi et Pilato, mors ejus non profuit, sed multum obfuit; non idcirco solum quod in morte Domini conspiraverant, sed ideo, quia bonum gratis odio habuerant, et poenitere neglexerant.
chunks.english: And although he died for sinners, his death did not profit Annas and Caiaphas, Herod and Pilate. Rather, it harmed them greatly. This was not only because they conspired in the Lord's death, but because they hated goodness without cause and refused to repent.
chunks.notes: References John 15:25

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Caeterum electi quamplures, in nece Christi, quamvis ignoranter, consenserant, pro quibus ipse et in cruce oravit, dicens: Pater, ignosce illis, quia nesciunt quid faciunt ; et sanguinem post credendo biberunt, quem prius saeviendo fuderunt.
chunks.english: But many of the elect consented to Christ's death, though ignorantly. For these he prayed even on the cross, saying, Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing. And later, by believing, they drank the blood that they had earlier shed in violence.
chunks.notes: References Luke 23:34 and Gregory's Moralia in Job 13.11

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cum malefacta hominum Deo nihil noceant, et eorum bene gesta nihil conferant: cur eos post mortem segregat, et non omnes pariter et aequaliter in unum locat?
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Since human evil deeds do not harm God, and their good deeds do not benefit him, why does he separate people after death? Why doesn't he place them all together equally in one place?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Rationi justitiae repugnat, ut justi cum injustis, et econtra, locum obtineant, . . . . . . . et multipliciter variando totam picturam distinguit.
chunks.english: MASTER: It contradicts the principle of justice that the just should occupy the same place as the unjust, or vice versa. God distinguishes the whole picture through manifold variation.
chunks.notes: Text appears corrupted with ellipsis in original

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ideo, sicut pictor opus suum coloribus variat, sic Deus regnum suum discretis ordinibus egregie clarificat. Quis enim esset decor picturae, si laquear totum cooperiret uno colore? nec pictura etiam posset dici.
chunks.english: Therefore, just as a painter varies his work with colors, so God magnificently illuminates his kingdom with distinct orders. For what beauty would a painting have if the whole ceiling were covered with one color? Indeed, it could not even be called a painting.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nunc diversos colores diversis locis pingit: et sic opus suum visui delectabile reddit. Sic insignitor lapidum varias gemmas diversis locis imprimit, et sic opus suum pretiosius efficit.
chunks.english: But now he paints different colors in different places, and thus makes his work delightful to the sight. Similarly, the jeweler sets various gems in different places, and thus makes his work more precious.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Ad quid ultimum judicium restat?
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Why does a final judgment remain to come?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Justitiae ratio exigit, ut judicium fiat; ut qui nunc se hypocrisi occultant, iniqui appareant; et qui nunc publice in flagitiis gloriantur, dignis tunc suppliciis juste subdantur;
chunks.english: MASTER: The principle of justice demands that judgment be made. Those who now hide themselves in hypocrisy will appear as wicked. And those who now publicly glory in their crimes will then justly be subjected to fitting punishments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: et qui leges contemnunt, justitiam despiciunt, pauperes et justos opprimunt, et per potentiam securi sunt, de rapinis florent, de damno aliorum gaudent, justis insultant, verba Dei calcant, pro talibus et his similibus laudantur, per terrorem ab hominibus honorantur:
chunks.english: Those who despise laws, scorn justice, oppress the poor and the just, who feel secure through their power, who flourish through robbery, rejoice in others' harm, insult the just, trample God's words, who are praised for such things and similar deeds, who are honored by people through fear:

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: hi, inquam, tunc a justis hic oppressis, et gaudio sequestrentur, et injustis in supplicio consocientur. Justi autem qui nunc injuste oppressi sunt, releventur; et qui bene viventes egerunt, remunerentur; et qui propter justitiam in opprobrio erant, honorentur.
chunks.english: these, I say, will then be separated from joy and united with the unjust in punishment. But the just who are now unjustly oppressed will be raised up. Those who lived and acted well will be rewarded. And those who were in disgrace for the sake of justice will be honored.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Nunc, cur in paradiso positus sit homo, cum ibi permansurus non esset, edicito.
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Now tell me why man was placed in paradise when he was not going to remain there.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Protoplastus ad beatitudinem creatus, in paradiso, id est, in loco voluptatis, locatus erat: sed, quia praeceptum Domini servare noluit, ideo contemptor juste exsilium subiit;
chunks.english: MASTER: The first man was created for blessedness and placed in paradise, that is, in a place of delight. But because he refused to keep the Lord's command, he justly suffered exile as a rebel.
chunks.notes: 'Protoplastus' means 'first-formed' - Adam

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: qui si nihil de paradisi deliciis gustasset, sed acceptum mandatum violasset, nunquam forsitan poenitentiam egisset, et sic semper exsul foret. Postquam vero expertam paradisi dulcedinem, sed cito amissam, ad memoriam revocavit, gravi se poenitentia multavit. Ideo rediit, et ideo reditum meruit.
chunks.english: If he had tasted nothing of paradise's delights but had violated the command he received, perhaps he would never have repented and would thus have remained an exile forever. But after he recalled to memory the sweetness of paradise that he had experienced but quickly lost, he punished himself with heavy repentance. Therefore he returned, and therefore he merited return.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De cujus mox utero, quasi de quodam castello, geminus exercitus, electorum scilicet et reproborum, prodiit; et acta hac re, certamen implacabili discordia iniit.
chunks.english: From his womb, as if from a certain fortress, a double army soon came forth, namely of the elect and the reprobate. And when this was done, they joined battle with implacable discord.
chunks.notes: 'His womb' refers to Adam as progenitor of humanity

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In hac itaque pugna magna in utrumque certatur, et victores quidem laureati triumphantes astra petunt: victi autem confusi, ad ima barathri descendunt.
chunks.english: In this great battle, the struggle goes both ways. The victors, crowned and triumphant, seek the stars. But the vanquished, in confusion, descend to the depths of hell.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sta etiam hic, et considera utrorumque instantiam.
chunks.english: Stand here now and consider the determination of both sides.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Citizens of Jerusalem Walk in Humility
chunks.latin: De civibus Jerusalem humilibus.
chunks.english: Concerning the humble citizens of Jerusalem.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquis per viam humilitatis, ab infantia incedit, et usque ad senilem aetatem, semper in melius proficit. Omnes homines superiores sibi putat; se vero inferiorem omnibus aestimat. Omnium actus laudat; suos reprobat.
chunks.english: Someone walks the path of humility from childhood, and up to old age always advances toward what is better. He considers all people superior to himself, and judges himself inferior to everyone. He praises all others' actions, but condemns his own.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De cive Jerusalem casto.
chunks.english: Concerning the chaste citizen of Jerusalem.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius castitatis semitam arripit, magna custodia sensus suos munit, et tamen omnes alios sanctos computat, et se velut immundum judicat.
chunks.english: Another seizes the path of chastity, guarding his senses with great care. And yet he counts all others as holy and judges himself as unclean.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De patiente.
chunks.english: Concerning the patient one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius patientiae callem calcat, universa dura et aspera pro Christo patienter tolerat, alios mansuetos reputat, seipsum immilem cogitat.
chunks.english: Another treads the way of patience, patiently enduring all harsh and difficult things for Christ. He considers others gentle, but thinks himself harsh.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De abstinente.
chunks.english: Concerning the abstinent one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius per abstinentiae iter graditur; magna castigatione carnis maceratur: et tamen alios in abstinentia districtiores honorando aestimat se ut voracem damnat.
chunks.english: Another walks the path of abstinence and wastes away through great mortification of the flesh. Yet in honor he considers others stricter in abstinence, and condemns himself as gluttonous.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De charitatem habente.
chunks.english: Concerning the one who has charity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius per latitudinem charitatis incedens, non solum amicos in Deo, sed etiam inimicos propter Deum diligit; contumelia accepta non solum poscenti veniam, injuriam laxat, sed etiam ipse reconciliari festinat.
chunks.english: Another, walking in the breadth of charity, loves not only friends in God but even enemies for God's sake. When he suffers insult, he not only forgives the injury when the offender seeks pardon, but he himself hastens to be reconciled.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec et talia sunt civium Jerusalem itinera, quibus ab exsilio properant ad aeterna tabernacula.
chunks.english: These and such like are the paths of the citizens of Jerusalem, by which they hasten from exile to the eternal dwelling places.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Citizens of Babylon and the Reprobate
chunks.latin: De civibus Babyloniae et de reprobis.
chunks.english: Concerning the citizens of Babylon and the reprobate.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verte te ad cives Babyloniae, et vide quales sint, per quas tendunt, plateae.
chunks.english: Turn now to the citizens of Babylon and see what kind they are, and by what streets they travel.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De luxurioso.
chunks.english: Concerning the lustful one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquis a primaeva aetate a luxuria inchoat; et in hac insatiabiliter usque ad decrepitum perdurat, nunquam quid jam fecerit, sed quid adhuc facere possit, pertractat.
chunks.english: Someone begins with lust from earliest youth and continues insatiably in it until decrepit old age, never considering what he has already done but only what he can still do.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De guloso.
chunks.english: Concerning the gluttonous one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius voracitati et ebrietati a pueritia se subjicit, et usque ad ultimam senectutem his insistit.
chunks.english: Another subjects himself to gluttony and drunkenness from boyhood and persists in these things until the very end of old age.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De rapacibus et crudelibus.
chunks.english: Concerning the rapacious and cruel.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Alius toto nisu rapere festinat; alius furtis invigilat; alius crudelitate pascitur; alius lucris non satiatur. Hic invidia tabescit; hic vero immunditia sordescit. Hic superbia erectus, cunctos despicit; hic odio plenus, cunctos mendaciis, et detractionibus afficit.
chunks.english: Another hastens to plunder with all his effort. Another keeps watch for opportunities to steal. Another feeds on cruelty. Another is never satisfied with gain. This one wastes away with envy, while that one grows foul with filth. This one, puffed up with pride, despises everyone. That one, full of hatred, afflicts everyone with lies and slander.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tales et tales sunt plateae reproborum, quibus irrevocabiliter festinant ad profunda inferorum.
chunks.english: Such are the streets of the reprobate, by which they hasten irrevocably to the depths of hell.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Different Ways Citizens Fight Each Other
chunks.latin: De diverso modo pugnandi civium Hierusalem, contra cives Babyloniae, et econtra.
chunks.english: Concerning the different manner of fighting between the citizens of Jerusalem and the citizens of Babylon, and vice versa.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aspice nunc etiam acies diverso modo ad pugnam instructas.
chunks.english: Look now at the battle lines drawn up for combat in different ways.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Reprobi justorum verba et opera abominantur, et consortia eorum detestantur, et refugiunt omne eorum consilium. Saepe eos callide circumveniunt; saepius bona eorum fraudulenter aut etiam violenter diripiunt; saepissime eos verberibus, vel etiam cruciatibus affligunt, aut in membrorum truncatione, vel variis suppliciis interimunt.
chunks.english: The reprobate abominate the words and works of the just, detest their company, and flee from all their counsel. Often they craftily entrap them. More often they fraudulently or even violently seize their goods. Most often they afflict them with beatings or tortures, or kill them by mutilating their limbs or inflicting various punishments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Econtra justi reproborum mores, ut grave pondus, sufferunt; de miserabili illorum conversatione gemunt; contagia eorum, quantum possunt, devitant; citius ab illorum contubernio liberari optant; pro eorum salute Deo supplicant; necessaria quae possunt, eis subministrant.
chunks.english: On the other hand, the just endure the ways of the reprobate as a heavy burden. They groan over their miserable manner of life. They avoid their contamination as much as they can. They desire to be freed from association with them as soon as possible. They pray to God for their salvation. They provide them with necessities as they are able.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Harmony Among Jerusalem Versus Discord in Babylon
chunks.latin: De bona concordia civium Jerusalem, et mala concordia civium Babyloniae.
chunks.english: Concerning the good harmony of the citizens of Jerusalem and the evil harmony of the citizens of Babylon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adhuc aliud considera. Electi omnes in bono concordant, et bonum quod non praevalent facere, in aliis amant.
chunks.english: Consider yet another thing. The elect all agree in goodness, and they love in others the good that they themselves cannot accomplish.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Reprobi vero, in malo omnes concordes sunt; in bono semper discordes existunt, et si alicui illorum ab aliquo justorum, forte increpationis vel admonitionis offertur verbum, omnes resistunt; omnes pariter contradicunt;
chunks.english: But the reprobate are all in agreement about evil. In good things they are always discordant. And if perhaps a word of reproof or admonition is offered to any of them by any of the just, they all resist. They all contradict together.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ipsi etiam qui non faciunt, quia bona quae facere nolunt, in aliis odio habent, moleste ferunt. Ideo eum omnes, etiam quem non noverunt, dignum odio dicunt, mendaciis detrahunt, injuriis lacessunt.
chunks.english: Even those who do not themselves do evil things, because they hate in others the good things they refuse to do, take it badly. Therefore they all declare worthy of hatred even the person they do not know. They slander him with lies and provoke him with injuries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Contemplare diligenter, et videbis in isto toto certamine, semper Cain paratum in acie contra Abel stare; et Ismael armatum adversus Isaac; Esau pugnare contra Jacob; Saul resistere David; Judam in Dominum, et Simonem Magum irruere in Petrum.
chunks.english: Look carefully and you will see in this entire conflict Cain always standing ready in battle line against Abel, Ishmael armed against Isaac, Esau fighting against Jacob, Saul resisting David, Judas against the Lord, and Simon Magus rushing against Peter.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vide etiam in hoc agone: qualiter saepe reprobi cum electis currere proponunt, et aliquando diu currunt, et in itinere fatigati, deficiunt, et turpiter ut canes ad vomitum redeunt.
chunks.english: See also in this contest how often the reprobate propose to run with the elect. Sometimes they run for a long time, but then, weary on the journey, they fail and shamefully return to their vomit like dogs.
chunks.notes: References Proverbs 26:11 and 2 Peter 2:22

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Particular Citizens of Babylon: Princes and Judges
chunks.latin: De civibus Babyloniae in particulari, et primo de his qui sunt principes et judices super alios.
chunks.english: Concerning the citizens of Babylon in particular, and first concerning those who are princes and judges over others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Veni huc ad supercilium montis, unde cuncta aedificia conspicere possis damnatae civitatis, Intuere principes et judices. Ecce posita est in eis bestiae sedes.
chunks.english: Come here to the brow of the mountain where you can survey all the buildings of the condemned city. Look at the princes and judges. Behold, in them is placed the throne of the beast.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omni tempore sunt ad malum intenti, semper in negotiis iniquitatis inexplebiliter occupati flagitia non solum faciunt, sed et alios facere instruunt, sancta vendunt, scelera emunt, totis viribus laborant, ne soli ad tartara veniant.
chunks.english: At all times they are intent on evil, always occupied insatiably in the business of iniquity. They not only commit crimes themselves, but teach others to do so. They sell holy things and buy crimes. They labor with all their strength lest they come to hell alone.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Clergy and Priests of Babylon
chunks.latin: De clericis et sacerdotibus Babyloniae.
chunks.english: Concerning the clergy and priests of Babylon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verte te ad clerum, et videbis in eis bestiae tentorium. Dei servitium negligunt; terrenis lucris inserviunt; sacerdotium per immunditiam polluunt; populum per simulationem seducunt; Deum per mala opera abnegant;
chunks.english: Turn to the clergy and you will see in them the tent of the beast. They neglect the service of God. They serve earthly gain. They pollute the priesthood through filth. They seduce the people through pretense. They deny God through evil works.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: omnes Scripturas ad salutem pertinentes abdicant; omnibus modis se laqueum et ruinam populo offerunt; quem caecum ipsi caeci ad interitum praeeunt.
chunks.english: They reject all the Scriptures that pertain to salvation. In every way they offer themselves as a snare and ruin to the people. Blind themselves, they lead the blind people before them to destruction.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De monachis Babyloniae.
chunks.english: Concerning the monks of Babylon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Contemplare etiam monachorum conciliabuta, et videbis in eis bestiae tabernacula. Per fictam professionem Deum irridentes iram ejus provocant; normam ejus regularem moribus et vita calcant; per habitum saeculum fallunt.
chunks.english: Look also at the gatherings of monks, and you will see in them the tabernacles of the beast. By their false profession they mock God and provoke his anger. They trample his regular rule with their behavior and life. They deceive the world through their habit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: multos ipsi decepti decipiunt; saecularibus negotiis implicati sunt, id est in servitio Dei desides existunt; plerique illorum gulae et illecebris dediti sunt; quidam immunditiae sordibus computrescunt.
chunks.english: Deceived themselves, they deceive many others. They are entangled in worldly business, that is, they are lazy in God's service. Many of them are devoted to gluttony and pleasures. Some rot in the filth of impurity.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De monialibus Babyloniae.
chunks.english: Concerning the nuns of Babylon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Prospice etiam habitacula monialium, et cernes in eis bestiae praeparatum thalamum. Hae a tenera aetate impudicitiam discunt; complices sibi quamplurimas ad cumulum suae damnationis asciscunt;
chunks.english: Look also at the dwellings of nuns, and you will see in them the prepared bridal chamber of the beast. From tender youth they learn shamelessness. They gather many accomplices for the increase of their damnation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: velo se operire festinant, quo magis frena luxuriae laxare queant omnibus fornicariis; stercore immunditiae implentur; hae mentes juvenum illaqueant et gaudent, si plures decipiant; et haec vult habere palmam victoriae, quae aliis praevalet inscelere.
chunks.english: They hasten to cover themselves with a veil so that they may more freely loosen the reins of lust with all fornicators. They are filled with the filth of impurity. They ensnare the minds of young men and rejoice if they deceive many. And she wishes to have the palm of victory who surpasses others in wickedness.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De communi plebe civitatis Babyloniae.
chunks.english: Concerning the common people of the city of Babylon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verte te ad reliquam plebem, et invenies bestiae effigiem. Sacerdotes despiciunt; de Deo quidquam audire contemnunt; totum tempus vitae in vanitate et jactantia ducunt, et ad omne opus suum reprobi sunt.
chunks.english: Turn to the rest of the common people and you will find the image of the beast. They despise priests. They scorn to hear anything about God. They spend their whole lifetime in vanity and boasting, and they are reprobate for every good work.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vulgus quoque indoctum bestiae habet idolum; Deum verum non noscunt; Deo ventri tota intentione deserviunt; per varia carnis desideria diffluunt, et per omnia vitam bestialem ducunt.
chunks.english: The uneducated rabble also has the idol of the beast. They do not know the true God. They serve the god of the belly with their whole intention. They dissolve in various fleshly desires and in all things live a bestial life.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: De mulieribus Babyloniae.
chunks.english: Concerning the women of Babylon.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Veni huc ad hujus vallis proclivia, et videbis monstruosa mulieris conventicula. In his bestia omnes suas pompas et monstra posuit; et has sua arma ferre constituit.
chunks.english: Come here to the slopes of this valley and you will see the monstrous gatherings of women. In them the beast has placed all his pomp and monstrosities, and he has appointed them to bear his weapons.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vides qualiter juvenes per luxuriam illaqueantur. Illa multos veneno enecat; haec viri vitam pro auro perdit; haec partus suos occidit; haec lites provocat; altera bella instigat.
chunks.english: You see how young men are ensnared through lust. This woman kills many with poison. That one destroys her husband's life for gold. This one murders her children. That one provokes quarrels. Another instigates wars.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ista maleficiis mentes hominum alienat; hanc vero nullus pecunia aut luxuria satiat; videndo multos decipit, illa flendo plerosque seducit: haec sunt hujus civitatis propugnacula; et haec sunt bestiae jacula.
chunks.english: This one alienates men's minds through sorceries. That one is satisfied by no amount of money or pleasure. One deceives many by her appearance, another seduces very many by her tears. These are the fortifications of this city, and these are the weapons of the beast.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. O Deus, quanta prodigiosa conspicio.
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Oh God, how many monstrosities I see!

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Moenia civitatis Babyloniae vidisti, destructionem quoque ejuslibet intueri.
chunks.english: MASTER: You have seen the walls of the city of Babylon. Now look at the destruction of each part.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nunc cives Babylonii, cum sint plures numero, civibus Hierusalem semper inferunt bellum, qui apud eos in exsilio positi sunt.
chunks.english: Now the citizens of Babylon, though they are more numerous, always wage war against the citizens of Jerusalem who are placed among them in exile.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postmodum vero, rex coelestis Hierusalem Deus, cum exercitu angelorum veniens, hanc perditam civitatem funditus subvertet, et electos suos inde liberans, in coeleste palatium secum adducet, quibus tale nunc spectaculum praebet.
chunks.english: But afterward, God the king of heavenly Jerusalem, coming with the army of angels, will utterly overthrow this ruined city. He will free his elect from it and bring them with him into the heavenly palace, to whom he now presents this spectacle.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hanc quam vides Babyloniam, id est, hujus mundi gloriam, cum suo principe diabolo, et omnibus hujus civitatis civibus, scilicet hujus mundi amatoribus, repente in stagno ignis et sulphuris merget; et tunc omnia in meliorem statum permutabit.
chunks.english: This Babylon that you see, that is, the glory of this world, together with its prince the devil and all the citizens of this city, namely the lovers of this world, he will suddenly plunge into the lake of fire and sulfur. And then he will change all things to a better state.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Magnum spectaculum praebuisti omnibus haec legentibus; ideo illi spectaculo te interesse concedat Deus. Et qua de causa erunt justi in gloria?
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: You have provided a great spectacle for all who read these things. May God grant that you be present at that spectacle. And for what reason will the just be in glory?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quia si sine fine viverent, semper justi esse vellent. Justitia igitur exigit ut semper beati sint, qui semper justitiam amplexati sunt.
chunks.english: MASTER: Because if they were to live without end, they would always wish to be just. Justice therefore requires that those who have always embraced justice should always be blessed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Quae autem est causa, quod injusti semper erunt in poena?
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: But what is the reason that the unjust will always be in punishment?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quia sine fine vellent vivere, ut sine fine male facere possent. Igitur justitia poscit, ut nunquam supplicio careant, qui nunquam justitiam volebant.
chunks.english: MASTER: Because they would wish to live without end so that they could do evil without end. Therefore justice demands that those who never desired justice should never be free from punishment.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur non possunt justi post resurrectionem peccare.
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Why cannot the just sin after the resurrection?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quia sic liberum arbitrium eorum confirmatum est, quod nihil mali volant; cum aequales angelis erunt, et ideo in perpetuum beati erunt.
chunks.english: MASTER: Because their free will is so confirmed that they desire nothing evil. They will be equal to the angels, and therefore they will be blessed forever.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Cur injusti ad bonum converti non poterunt?
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Why will the unjust not be able to turn to good?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: MAG. Quia bonum semper odio habent et ideo miseri in perpetuum erunt.
chunks.english: MASTER: Because they always hold good in hatred, and therefore they will be miserable forever.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Summary of the Book's Main Teachings
chunks.latin: Conclusio sive recapitulatio hujus libri.
chunks.english: Conclusion or summary of this book.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ut igitur nostrae disputationis sententiam brevi epilogo concludam.
chunks.english: Therefore, to conclude the teaching of our disputation with a brief summary:

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Propositum Dei est: Numerum electorum ex angelis et hominibus perfici. Praedestinatio: Justos in beatitudine semper gloriari, injustos semper in miseria cruciari.
chunks.english: God's purpose is to complete the number of the elect from angels and humans. Predestination is that the just always glory in blessedness, and the unjust always suffer torment in misery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praescientia vel providentia: Futurarum rerum semper praesens Dei inspectio. Necessitas: Hominem post peccata mori.
chunks.english: Foreknowledge or providence is God's ever-present vision of future things. Necessity is that man must die after sins.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Justitia: Rectum velle. Arbitrium: Rectae justitiae volendi judicium. Libertas: Servandi justitiam potestas, vel arbitrium nolendi volendi judicium.
chunks.english: Justice is to will what is right. Free will is the judgment of willing right justice. Freedom is the power to preserve justice, or the judgment of willing and not willing by choice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Gratia: Boni inspiratio vel mali liberatio. Meritum: Perseverantia justitiae. Praemium summum: Beatitudo. Supplicium: aeterna miseria.
chunks.english: Grace is the inspiration of good or liberation from evil. Merit is perseverance in justice. The highest reward is blessedness. Punishment is eternal misery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia quosdam infantes utpote baptizatos, sola sua justitia non damnari est inevitabile; et quosdam provectae aetatis, justitiae Dei per liberum arbitrium spretores justitia dictante, debitam poenam evadere, est inevitabile, propter hoc, nomen huic libello imponitur.
chunks.english: And because it is inevitable that certain infants, being baptized, are not damned by God's justice alone, and that certain adults, despisers of God's justice through free will, cannot escape due punishment as justice dictates, for this reason the title is given to this little book:

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: Inevitabile.
chunks.english: The Inevitable.
chunks.notes: This is the title of the work

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec de praedestinatione, praescientia et libero arbitrio offero filiis Ecclesiae, quia ea despicient filii Babyloniae.
chunks.english: These things concerning predestination, foreknowledge, and free will I offer to the children of the Church, because the children of Babylon will despise them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quicunque hoc contempserit vel impugnaverit, se de numero praedestinatorum non esse demonstrabit. Qui vero quaestionem post haec de libero arbitrio moverit, caecus clara die in mortem offendit.
chunks.english: But whoever despises or attacks this work will demonstrate that he is not among the number of the predestined. And whoever raises questions about free will after these matters is like a blind man who stumbles to his death in broad daylight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: DISC. Benedictus Deus qui hoc inspiravit fratribus ut vellent me ad te dirigere; quatenus haec mira mererer a tuo mellifluo ore percipere.
chunks.english: DISCIPLE: Blessed be God who inspired the brothers to send me to you, so that I might deserve to receive these wonderful things from your honeyed mouth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et revera noveris, quod greges reproborum pro hoc venerando opere, magno te odio abominabuntur; et maxime ob hoc quod eosdem per liberum arbitrium, filios vitiorum, imo daemonum probaveris, detestabuntur.
chunks.english: And truly you should know that the flocks of the reprobate will abominate you with great hatred for this venerable work, and especially because you have proven them to be sons of vices, indeed of demons, through their free will. They will detest you.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magnas autem grates tibi persolvet gloriosus coetus ad vitam praedestinatorum, quod tam mirabile opus prompsisti in laudem ipsorum.
chunks.english: But the glorious company of those predestined to life will render you great thanks because you have brought forth such a wonderful work in their praise.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et, sicut te audivi dicere, hoc quod est a Deo praedestinatum, ut quidquid electi juste petierint, eis tribuatur; magnis precibus Dei clementiam exorant ut te ultima dies in consortio illorum inveniat. Amen.
chunks.english: And, as I have heard you say, since it is predestined by God that whatever the elect justly ask will be granted to them, they beseech God's mercy with great prayers that your last day may find you in their fellowship. Amen.
